Official name: Stop Spying Bosses Act
Casual name: Stop Spying Bosses Act
Short summary: Requires employers to disclose AI-driven workplace surveillance details and prohibits using AI to predict non-work behavior. Establishes a Privacy and Technology Division for oversight. Enables private rights of action and whistleblower protections. Mandates coordination with federal and state agencies.
Long summary:
Prohibits employers from using workplace surveillance to monitor labor activities, ascertain political or religious views, or determine health or immigration status unrelated to job duties.

Requires employers to disclose details about workplace surveillance practices, including data collection methods, frequency, storage locations, business purposes, and third-party transfers, to employees and applicants.

Establishes a Privacy and Technology Division within the Department of Labor to oversee compliance and issue regulations.

Allows employees to access, correct, and request data collected about them and prohibits data transfer to third parties without disclosure and cybersecurity measures.

Grants employees and labor organizations the right to bring civil actions against violations, with potential damages and injunctive relief.

Prohibits predispute arbitration agreements regarding surveillance-related disputes.

Mandates annual reports to Congress on workplace surveillance impacts and recommendations for mitigating harms.

Coordinates with federal and state agencies to ensure consistent regulatory treatment of workplace surveillance.

Official name: Next Generation Pipelines Research and Development Act, Section 4 ("Advanced pipeline materials and technologies demonstration initiative")
Casual name: H. R. 7073 Pipelines R&D Act, Section 4 ("Advanced pipeline materials and technologies demonstration initiative")
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Energy to establish a demonstration initiative to advance pipeline and related infrastructure technologies, such as autonomous sensor systems; advanced computational, data analytics, and machine learning models; and autonomous robotic and patch technologies. Requires the Secretary of Energy to evaluate projects' technical performance and economic feasibility during the selection process.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Energy to establish a demonstration initiative awarding financial assistance for demonstration projects, of low- to mid-technology readiness, for pipelines and associated infrastructure technologies.

Declares advanced sensor technologies such as autonomous sensor systems, including uncrewed aircraft, eligible for financial assistance through the initiative.

Declares advanced computational, data analytics, and machine learning models, as well as autonomous robotic and patch technologies, eligible for financial assistance through the initiative.

Requires the Secretary of Energy to evaluate projects' technical performance and economic feasibility, for return on investment impact, in the selection process for the initiative. 

Authorizes $45 million for fiscal year 2025 and $50 million annually for fiscal years 2026-2029 from designated offices to implement the initiative.

Terminates the initiative five years after enactment.

Official name: Diversify Tech Act
Casual name: Diversify Tech Act
Short summary: Establishes a task force within the Department of Commerce to promote diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility (DEIA) in the tech industry. Evaluates AI's role in DEIA, monitors minority treatment, and reports on layoffs and DEIA progress.
Long summary:
Establishes a Tech Industry Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility (DEIA) Task Force within the Department of Commerce.

Mandates the Task Force to conduct audits on minority representation, hiring policies, and layoff trends in the tech industry.

Requires submission of an initial audit report to Congress within 60 days of the Task Force’s establishment.

Obliges the Task Force to promote DEIA, offer guidance to tech entities, and consult with stakeholders on DEIA issues.

Tasks the Task Force with advising on outreach programs and encouraging venture capital investment in minority-owned startups.

Mandates annual reports evaluating tech industry progress on DEIA goals, the impact of AI on discrimination, and proposing policy solutions for accountability.

Requires a special report on how 2023 tech layoffs affected DEIA, including potential violations of federal laws.

Official name: Small Business Broadband and Emerging Information Technology Enhancement Act of 2023
Casual name: S. 2677 Small Business Technology Enhancement Act
Short summary: Designates a coordinator to assist small businesses in adopting AI and emerging technologies, requiring collaboration with federal agencies. Mandates biennial reports on broadband's impact on small businesses, and updates training for SBA employees on AI technologies.
Long summary:
Designates a senior employee of the Office of Investment and Innovation as the broadband and emerging information technology coordinator to assist small businesses in adopting broadband and emerging technologies.

Requires the coordinator to work with the SBA's chief information officer, chief technology officer, and other relevant federal agencies.

Mandates the coordinator to identify best practices and catalog tools and training for small businesses on broadband and emerging technologies.

Obligates the coordinator to provide training to SBA employees on these technologies and relevant federal programs.

Requires biennial reports on the SBA's activities related to broadband and emerging technologies.

Instructs the Chief Counsel for Advocacy to study and report on the impact of broadband availability, speed, and price, and the deployment of emerging technologies on small businesses.

Amends the Small Business Act to include provisions for increasing small business access to broadband and emerging technologies.

Official name: Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023
Casual name: Algorithmic Accountability Act of 2023
Short summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to issue rules requiring “covered entities” to conduct, document, and submit impact assessments for automated decision systems used in critical decisions (e.g., jobs, credit, housing, health, education). Requires initial and annual summary reports, stakeholder consultation, testing for performance and fairness, and mitigation of likely material harms resulting from automated decision systems. Creates an FTC Bureau of Technology, builds a public repository of summaries, redefines violations under the FTC Act, and authorizes state attorney general enforcement.
Long summary:
Directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to issue regulations requiring covered entities to conduct and document impact assessments for automated decision systems (ADS) and augmented critical decision processes that materially affect consumers in areas such as employment, education, housing, healthcare, and finance.

Requires covered entities to perform pre-deployment and ongoing evaluations addressing privacy, security, reliability, fairness, and potential discriminatory or harmful impacts, and to mitigate any material negative effects identified.

Mandates stakeholder consultation with internal ethics and technology teams and external experts, advocates, and impacted communities during system design, testing, and deployment.

Requires covered entities to maintain detailed documentation and submit both initial and annual summary reports to the FTC in standardized, machine-readable formats describing system purpose, data sources, performance metrics, fairness testing, and mitigation measures.

Directs the FTC to establish a public, searchable repository containing non-confidential information from summary reports and to publish annual aggregate findings on the use and oversight of automated decision systems.

Establishes a Bureau of Technology within the FTC to oversee implementation, technical analysis, and enforcement, supported by additional staff and resources.

Treats violations as unfair or deceptive acts or practices (UDAP) under the FTC Act, authorizes state attorneys general to bring civil enforcement actions, and encourages coordination with other federal and state regulators.

Preserves existing state, tribal, and local laws while promoting consistent national oversight of algorithmic decision-making systems.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Allied Collaboration for Crucial Operations, Research, and Development Act of 2024
Casual name: AI ACCORD Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to submit a strategy for enhancing AI-driven defense collaboration with Middle Eastern allies. Establishes a forum for combatant commands to coordinate AI tools and training. Mandates reporting to Congress on progress and recommendations for legislative action.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Defense to submit a strategy to Congress within 180 days to enhance cooperation with Middle Eastern allies using partner-sharing network capabilities for joint defense.

Requires the strategy to include a summary of ongoing efforts, challenges, and recommended actions for implementing joint partner-sharing networks.

Directs the Chief Data and Artificial Intelligence Officer of the Department of Defense to establish a forum for warfighters to coordinate on AI tools, methodologies, and training within 180 days.

Specifies that the forum should identify AI use cases, categorize risks, and prioritize AI tools while considering training shortfalls and opportunities for cooperation with allies.

Obligates the Secretary of Defense to report progress on the forum's establishment and recommend legislative actions to Congress within one year.

Official name: Strengthening Consumer Protections and Medical Debt Transparency Act
Casual name: Medical Debt Transparency Act
Short summary: Establishes a public database for medical debt collection, requiring health care entities to report debt collection practices. Prohibits extraordinary collection actions during pending insurance appeals or before determining patient eligibility for assistance. Requires detailed patient billing information and imposes penalties for non-compliance.
Long summary:
Amends the Public Health Service Act to prohibit health care entities and their debt collectors from initiating extraordinary collection actions if a health insurance appeal is pending.

Mandates health care entities to determine patients' eligibility for assistance programs before commencing extraordinary collection actions.

Prohibits extraordinary collection actions until 180 days after the initial bill is sent to the patient.

Requires health care entities to provide patients with itemized statements and detailed payment receipts, and information on language assistance services.

Establishes a public database for medical debt collection, requiring health care entities to submit detailed annual reports, including data on debt collection practices and patient demographics.

Imposes penalties for non-compliance, including actual damages and additional court-permitted amounts.

Requires the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to report on medical debt collections, incorporating data from the public database and other sources.

Official name: Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023
Casual name: Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023
Short summary: Designates the Secretary of Commerce as principal advisor on blockchain and AI policy. Requires development of policies addressing AI-related risks in blockchain. Promotes U.S. leadership and competitiveness in blockchain and AI integration. Establishes advisory committees and reports to Congress.
Long summary:
Designates the Secretary of Commerce as the principal advisor to the President on blockchain technology and related applications.

Requires the Secretary to develop policies and recommendations addressing issues such as decentralized identity, cybersecurity, AI, regulatory compliance, and fraud reduction.

Instructs the Secretary to promote U.S. leadership and competitiveness in blockchain technology through a Blockchain Deployment Program.

Mandates the establishment of advisory committees with diverse stakeholders to support blockchain adoption.

Directs the Secretary to facilitate the development and dissemination of best practices for blockchain technology.

Obligates the Secretary to consult with private sector stakeholders and disseminate research on blockchain technology.

Requires annual reports to Congress on activities, recommendations for legislation, and emerging risks and trends in blockchain technology.

Terminates the Blockchain Deployment Program seven years after enactment.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Public Awareness and Education Campaign Act
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Public Awareness and Education Campaign Act
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Commerce to launch a nationwide public education campaign on artificial intelligence. On the campaign, they must explain its benefits, risks, and everyday uses. They must also promote public understanding of AI-generated media and fraud risks, and call for reporting key performance indicators to Congress.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Commerce to create and lead a nationwide public awareness and education campaign about artificial intelligence, focusing on explaining what AI is, how it affects people’s daily lives, and the benefits and risks that come with using it.

Directs the Secretary to work with other federal agencies, including the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the National Telecommunications and Information Administration (NTIA), to make sure the information shared with the public is accurate and up to date.

Instructs the Secretary to set clear goals and measurable results, known as key performance indicators, to track the campaign's effectiveness and gather data that demonstrates its impact.

It also encourages targeted education for groups more likely to be victims of AI scams or fraud, such as older adults, helping them recognize and avoid AI-driven deception. and it promotes public understanding of how AI is used in everyday technology, such as predictive text, navigation apps, and voice assistants, as well as in business applications like fraud detection and financial trading.

Requires the Secretary to consult experts from universities, research groups, private companies, and community organizations to guide the campaign and ensure it reflects real-world AI developments. Lastly, it mandates that the Secretary report to Congress one year after starting the campaign, including data on its success and recommendations for improvement or future AI-related outreach.

Official name: Department of Peacebuilding Act of 2023, Sec. 107 ("Office of Technology for Peace")
Casual name: Department of Peacebuilding Act, Sec. 107 ("Office of Technology for Peace")
Short summary: Directs the Department of State to give grants to researchers and developers of AI for the public good as well as researchers who prioritize safety and fair use of AI
Long summary:
There shall be in the Department an Office of Technology for Peace,

The Department of State has a vested interest in ensuring Artificial Intelligence is used for the benefit of society. 

Offers grants to researchers and developers who find ways to ensure that AI is beneficial to society.

Emphasizes the Artificial Intelligence for non-malignant purposes in public health, transportation, and sustainable energy.

Official name: Computer Science for All Act of 2023
Casual name: Computer Science for All Act of 2023
Short summary: Expands access to computer science (CS) education nationwide, including instruction in AI and computational thinking. Authorizes the Department of Education to fund grants supporting AI-related curriculum, teaching training, and equitable participation in CS programs.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Education to award grants to states, local educational agencies, and eligible Tribal schools to expand computer science education, including instruction in artificial intelligence and computational thinking.

Mandates grant recipients to provide every high school student with access to computer science education within five years, ensure a progression of computer science education from prekindergarten through middle school, minimize enrollment and academic achievement gaps, and monitor and evaluate program activities.

Requires the use of grant funds for teacher training, expanding access to learning materials, and reducing equity gaps in STEAM education for underrepresented groups.

Permits grant funds to be used for building regional collaborations, hiring instructional personnel, and disseminating effective practices.

Limits use of grant funds for equipment purchases to 15%. Directs the Secretary to reserve at most 2.5 percent of funds available for grants under this section for national activities.

Authorizes $250 million for fiscal year 2024 and the following four fiscal years.

Requires grant recipients to report biannually on the use of funds and disclose student demographics.

Mandates the Secretary to report to Congress within five years on program expansion recommendations.

Official name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Casual name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to democratize access to AI resources, enhance U.S. AI research capacity, and foster diverse talent. Creates a steering subcommittee and program office to oversee governance, resource allocation, and compliance with privacy and ethical standards.
Long summary:
Establishes the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) to democratize access to AI research resources and spur innovation.

Creates a NAIRR Steering governance structure chaired by the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy to oversee governance and resource allocation.

Mandates the National Science Foundation to establish a Program Management Office to maintain day-to-day operations and ensure compliance.

Requires development of an Operating Entity through competitive processes to manage the NAIRR.

Ensures resources include computational resources, data repositories, educational tools, and AI testbeds.

Specifies criteria for user eligibility, emphasizing U.S.-based researchers and entities.

Imposes requirements and audits for privacy, ethics, civil rights, and security in AI research using NAIRR.

Authorizes a fee schedule with a free access tier to expand research participation.

Ensures adherence to national security, research integrity, and open-source principles.

Official name: Do Not Disturb Act
Casual name: Do Not Disturb Act
Short summary: This bill requires robocalls and text messages to clearly disclose at the start when artifical intelligence is being used to mimic a human voice so recipients know when they are intereacting with AI. the bill also gives double the penalties for scams that rely on AI impersonation.
Long summary:
The bill requires robocallers and telemarketers to clearly disclose artificial intelligence at the beginning of any call or text message when it is being use to mimic a human voice. This ensures that consumers are being informed and aware that they are interacting with AI rather than a real person. The bill also raises penalties for violations involving AI impersonations, the fines however are doubled if used AI to commit fraud, cause harm, or wrongfully obtain money. 

On the other hand, internet-based phone providers such as VoIP services are responsible for verifying the identities of their customers, monitoring call and texts that indicate any AI-driven ronocall ro telemarketing activity. Within this it must conduct investigations into suspecious behavior and reporting evidence of violation to the FCC to enforce the law. The bill then requires the FTC to revise existing telemarketing regulations to mandate clear discolure whenever AI is used in calls or texts.

Official name: Medicare Transaction Fraud Prevention Act
Casual name: Medicare Transaction Fraud Prevention Act
Short summary: Establishes a two-year pilot program using predictive risk-scoring algorithms for oversight of Medicare payments. Requires testing, evaluation, and review considering AI ethics guidelines. Authorizes human review of transactions flagged by algorithms. Permits intervention in high-risk transactions, including notifications and potential Medicare card terminations.
Long summary:
Establishes a pilot program under the Medicare program to test the use of a predictive risk-scoring algorithm for overseeing payments related to durable medical equipment and clinical diagnostic laboratory tests.

Requires the pilot to run for two years, starting no later than January 1, 2025, with voluntary beneficiary participation.

Mandates algorithm development in collaboration with industry representatives and modeled after real-time consumer feedback mechanisms, with scores ranging from 1 (least risky) to 99 (most risky).

Conditions use of the algorithm on prior testing and evaluation considering the AI Bill of Rights and requires notifying affected beneficiaries about data processing and implications.

Allows transaction review and possible suspension based on high-risk scores, offering beneficiaries the chance to dispute inaccuracies.

Requires that alerts for identified fraud risks be communicated to beneficiaries, along with guidance on reporting suspected fraud to law enforcement, and authorizes determination of Medicare card termination to prevent fraud.

Official name: No AI FRAUD Act
Casual name: No AI FRAUD Act
Short summary: Establishes property rights in likeness and voice, transferable post-mortem for ten years. Prohibits unauthorized cloning and replicas, imposing liabilities. Specifies damages per violation. Allows punitive damages and attorney fees. Provides First Amendment defense. Exempts negligible harm. Sets four-year limitations period.
Long summary:
Establishes property rights for individuals in their likeness and voice, treating them as intellectual property that is transferable and descendible for ten years post-mortem.

Prohibits unauthorized distribution, transmission, or availability of personalized cloning services, digital voice replicas, or digital depictions without the individual’s consent, imposing liabilities for violations.

Specifies that unauthorized use incurs damages of $50,000 per violation for cloning services and $5,000 per violation for voice replicas or depictions, plus actual damages and profits.

Allows for punitive damages and attorney fees for injured parties and invalidates disclaimers as a defense.

Provides a First Amendment defense, balancing public interest with intellectual property rights, and considers commercial use and impact on the value of the original work.

Exempts negligible harm from liability and defines harm to include financial, physical, and severe emotional distress.

Sets a four-year limitations period for civil actions and ensures no preemption of other rights or laws.

Official name: DOE and NSF Interagency Research Act
Casual name: DOE and NSF Interagency Research Act
Short summary: Facilitates collaborative AI research between the Department of Energy and National Science Foundation, focusing on machine learning, data analytics, and computational advancements. Requires interagency agreements and promotes data sharing, infrastructure support, and STEM education initiatives.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Energy (DOE) and the National Science Foundation (NSF) to coordinate research and development activities.

Mandates the establishment of a memorandum of understanding between the DOE and NSF, utilizing a competitive, merit-reviewed process for project applications.

Authorizes collaborative research in key areas including artificial intelligence, machine learning, data analytics, quantum computing, and computational sciences.

Promotes interagency collaboration and data sharing among Federal agencies, National Laboratories, and other institutions.

Supports research infrastructure development and STEM education initiatives, including internships and professional development.

Allows reimbursable agreements between the DOE and NSF to enhance research effectiveness.

Obligates the DOE and NSF to report on interagency coordination, research achievements, and future opportunities within two years of enactment.

Ensures activities comply with research security provisions under existing law.

Official name: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2023
Casual name: Combating Cartels on Social Media Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to assess and counter cartel recruitment on social media by developing a national strategy, consulting with operators and communities, creating reporting mechanisms, and designating an official within CBP to manage and share intelligence
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Homeland Security to assess within 180 days how transnational criminal organizations use covered services, including social media and other online platforms, for recruitment and other illicit activities, and to evaluate existing government and operator responses

Mandates submission of a National Strategy to combat illicit recruitment activity by Transnational Criminal Organizations on social media and online platforms within one year

Proposes improvements in cooperation between government entities and social media operators, enhanced intelligence analysis, and proactive law enforcement efforts, international partnerships, and targeted outreach to youth in border communities

Requires establishment of a centralized mechanism for reporting recruitment efforts by transnational criminal organizations to the Department of Homeland Security

Excludes any legislative recommendations not enacted by strategy submission date, including encryption policies and Section 230 reforms

Instructs the Secretary to consult with various federal, state, and local entities, as well as social media companies and experts during strategy development and implementation

Mandates semiannual reporting on the implementation of the strategy and its progress to appropriate congressional committees for five years

Designates a specific official within U.S. Customs and Border Protection to manage intelligence related to recruitment activities and to facilitate voluntary information reporting from social media operators

Official name: Countering Antisemitism Act, Sec. 8 (Online Antisemitism, Holocaust Denial, and Distortion)
Casual name: Countering Antisemitism Act, Sec. 8 (Online Antisemitism, Holocaust Denial, and Distortion)
Short summary: Requires the National Coordinator to conduct annual studies on online antisemitism, including Holocaust denial and distortion. Recommends transparency in algorithms, content moderation, and platform accountability. Submits reports to specific Senate and House committees for 10 years.
Long summary:
Mandates the National Coordinator to Counter Antisemitism to conduct a study on online antisemitism, including Holocaust denial and distortion, within 180 days of the Act's enactment and annually for 10 years.

Requires the study to analyze the prevalence of antisemitic content online.

Recommends to Congress measures to counter online antisemitism, focusing on transparency in algorithmic systems, content moderation, enforcement of community standards, and accountability for individuals and platforms.

Obligates the National Coordinator to submit the report to specific Senate and House committees.

Official name: Advanced Aviation Act
Casual name: Advanced Aviation Act
Short summary: Establishes the Office of Advanced Aviation, headed by an Associate Administrator, to oversee rulemaking, certification, and integration of advanced aviation systems, including AI-driven technologies, into national airspace. Requires interagency coordination and stakeholder engagement for safe and timely integration.
Long summary:
Redesignates the Office of NextGen as the Office of Advanced Aviation within 1 year.

Appoints an Associate Administrator to oversee the Office of Advanced Aviation.

Coordinates rulemaking and approval processes for advanced aviation systems' standardization, certification, safety, and integration into national airspace.

Establishes an Office of Advanced Integration to manage responsibilities related to advanced aviation systems.

Forms an Advanced Aviation Coordination Unit to promote safe integration and certification of advanced aviation systems.

Redesignates the William J. Hughes Technical Center as the William J. Hughes Technical Center for Advanced Aviation, responsible for research, development, and integration of new aviation technologies.

Establishes an Aviation Innovation Program to evaluate and integrate new aviation-related technologies into the national airspace system.

Defines "advanced aviation systems" to include unmanned aircraft systems, counter-UAS systems, powered lift aircraft, and related technologies.

Official name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act of 2024
Casual name: Small Business Artificial Intelligence Training Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Commerce to develop AI training resources for small businesses, including those in rural and underserved areas. Mandates annual review and updates of resources. Allows grants for AI training. Terminates the program after three years.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Commerce, in coordination with various agencies, to develop AI training resources for small businesses, including those in rural, Tribal, or underserved areas.

Requires these resources to cover topics such as prompt engineering, financial management, cybersecurity, and government contracting.

Obligates the review and update of these resources every year to ensure relevance and effectiveness.

Coordinates with the Small Business Administration for the distribution of these resources through its network, including development centers and business opportunity centers.

Allows for a grants program to fund training providers using the developed resources.

Demands initial and annual reports to Congress on the progress and impact of the training resources.

Establishes a sunset clause terminating the program three years after the Act's enactment.

Official name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2023, Sec. 14 ("Automation and Artificial Intelligence")
Casual name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act, Sec. 14 ("Automation and AI")
Short summary: Requires the Director to issue AI guidance for agency cybersecurity improvement. Mandates annual AI cybersecurity reports to Congress. Orders the Comptroller General to report on AI-related privacy and cybersecurity risks, and study AI's role in federal cybersecurity automation.
Long summary:
Define "information system" as per section 3502 of title 44, United States Code.

Mandates the Director to issue guidance on AI use by agencies to enhance cybersecurity of information systems.

Requires the Director and agency heads to consider AI systems where automation aids cybersecurity.

Obligates the Director to report annually for 5 years to Congress on AI use in enhancing cybersecurity.

Instructs the Comptroller General to report to Congress on AI-associated privacy and cybersecurity risks within 2 years.

Directs the Comptroller General to study and report within 2 years on AI and automation use across the federal government for cybersecurity, including automated updates to cybersecurity tools and processes.

Official name: Protect Working Musicians Act of 2023
Casual name: Protect Working Musicians Act of 2023
Short summary: Limits liability under antitrust laws for Individual Music Creator Owners who collectively negotiate or refuse licensing to Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or companies developing or deploying generative AI. Specifies conditions for collective negotiations, ensuring consistency with antitrust laws.
Long summary:
Grants Independent Music Creator Owners immunity from antitrust liability for collectively negotiating music licensing terms with Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or developing or deploying generative AI.

Defines "generative artificial intelligence" as AI systems generating novel text, video, images, audio, or other media based on prompts.

Defines “Individual Music Creator Owner” as an individual or group of musicians, producers, mixers, or sound engineers who own their copyrights and either earn less than $1 million annually in licensing revenue or qualify as a small business.

Defines “Dominant Online Music Distribution Platform” as an online service for listening to sound recordings with over $100 million in annual music-related revenue, excluding entities eligible for certain statutory licenses.

Requires that collective negotiations among Independent Music Creator Owners be nondiscriminatory, not limited to price, and directly related to negotiations with Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms or generative AI companies.

Ensures that collective negotiations do not involve parties other than Independent Music Creator Owners, Dominant Online Music Distribution Platforms, or generative AI companies.

Stipulates that the Act does not modify, impair, or supersede existing antitrust laws beyond the specified provisions.
Tags: Applications: Broadcasting and media production;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Tiering

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 1024 ("Technology Review of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Technologies")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 1024 ("Review of AI and ML Technologies")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to conduct a review of current and planned AI and machine learning technologies to enhance airport efficiency and safety. Asks the administrator to consider best practices from previous applications of AI and Chinese use cases in enhancing airport operations.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator to conduct a review of current and planned AI and machine learning technologies to enhance airport efficiency and safety.

Asks the Administrator to consider best practices and lessons learned from domestic and international AI and machine learning applications.

Permits coordination with other Federal agencies to identify China's use of AI and machine learning in airport operations.

Mandates the review to include AI and machine learning applications in jet bridges, airport service vehicles, aircraft taxi, air traffic control operations, and other areas deemed necessary by the Administrator.

Obligates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress within one year detailing the review's findings.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Evaluation;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Evaluation;Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 1028 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 1028 ("Air Traffic Control Training")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to assess the benefits of deploying AI and other advanced technologies in enhancing air traffic controller training. Mandates the Administrator to submit a report to Congress on research findings within one year.
Long summary:
Mandates the Administrator to carry out a research program assessing the benefits of deploying advanced technologies such as AI to modernize, enhance, and streamline on-the-job training for FAA air traffic controllers.

Requires the Administrator to include collaboration with labor organizations and other stakeholders in the research program.

Mandates the Administrator to report findings to Congress within 1 year of the Act's enactment.
Tags: Applications: Transportation;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: REAL Political Advertisements Act
Casual name: REAL Political Advertisements Act
Short summary: Requires political advertisements containing images or video generated with artificial intelligence to include clear disclaimers. Directs the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to define AI-generated content, issue implementing regulations, and report on compliance and enforcement.
Long summary:
Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 to require any political advertisement containing images or video footage generated in whole or in part using artificial intelligence to include a clear and conspicuous disclaimer, with specific formatting requirements for text, audio, video, and other media to ensure visibility and audibility.

Directs the Federal Election Commission to promulgate regulations within 120 days of enactment to implement the disclaimer requirement and define "content generated by artificial intelligence" to reflect both current and emerging uses of generative AI.

Requires the FEC to submit biennial reports to Congress assessing compliance and enforcement, recommending modifications, and identifying further measures to enhance transparency and accountability in political advertising.

Official name: Facial Recognition Act of 2023
Casual name: Facial Recognition Act
Short summary: Regulates law enforcement use of facial recognition by requiring court orders, protecting civil rights, and mandating transparency. Prohibits reliance on facial recognition alone for arrests, requires accuracy and bias testing, and establishes reporting, auditing, and public notice requirements.
Long summary:
Prohibits law enforcement from using facial recognition without a court order, except in defined emergency circumstances. 

Mandates regular removal of photos from databases used for facial recognition, including minors and individuals not charged or acquitted.

Requires detailed applications from prosecutors and approval from courts for authorized use of facial recognition.

Prohibits the use of facial recognition with body cameras, for face surveillance, or for immigration enforcement.

Enforces annual accuracy and bias testing by independent entities and prohibits systems that fail to meet minimum standards. 

Requires public reporting and logging of facial recognition use, including demographic data.

Mandates audits of law enforcement agencies' use of facial recognition to ensure compliance and prevent misuse.

Official name: AI Incident Reporting and Security Enhancement Act
Casual name: AI Incident Reporting and Security Enhancement Act
Short summary: Directs the National Institute of Standards and Technology to update the National Vulnerability Database for AI vulnerabilities and assess voluntary reporting of significant AI security and safety incidents. Requires multi-stakeholder collaboration to evaluate and develop definitions, standards, guidelines, and reporting mechanisms for incident tracking.
Long summary:
Directs the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to update the National Vulnerability Database to include AI security vulnerabilities.

Requires NIST to establish common definitions for AI security vulnerabilities and develop standards for managing these vulnerabilities.

Mandates a multi-stakeholder process to assess voluntary reporting and tracking of substantial AI security and safety incidents, and to establish guidelines, best practices, and standardized mechanisms for reporting AI incidents.

Obligates NIST to submit a report to Congress within three years, detailing the findings and recommendations from the multi-stakeholder process on AI incident reporting and tracking.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Literacy Act of 2023
Casual name: H. R. 6791 (AI Literacy Act of 2023)
Short summary: Amends the Digital Equity Act to promote AI literacy by establishing grants for AI education in schools, community colleges, and higher education. Supports teacher training, lab development, and virtual learning platforms and requires annual Congressional reports on AI education initiatives.
Long summary:
Amends the Digital Equity Act of 2021 to include artificial intelligence (AI) literacy within the scope of digital literacy.

Defines "artificial intelligence literacy" as understanding basic AI principles, applications, implications, limitations, and ethical considerations.

Requires grants to support AI literacy initiatives in public elementary and secondary schools, including teacher training, professional development, and resource development.

Supports higher education institutions to develop AI learning labs, virtual platforms, and educational tools for teachers and community educators.

Includes grants to community anchor institutions for AI education and training programs.

Mandates an annual report to Congress summarizing and analyzing the progress and impact of AI literacy initiatives, with recommendations for expansion or modification.

Official name: AI Transparency in Elections Act of 2024
Casual name: AI Transparency in Elections Act of 2024
Short summary: Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act to require clear disclaimers on political ads using AI-generated content. Imposes penalties for non-compliance and mandates the Federal Election Commission to issue regulations and report compliance.
Long summary:
Requires the inclusion of disclaimers on political advertisements containing content substantially generated by AI.

Mandates that disclaimers must clearly state the use of AI in creating or materially altering the content.

Establishes safe harbor guidelines for what constitutes a "clear and conspicuous manner" in disclaimers for image, audio, and video communications.

Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 to include penalties for violations of the disclaimer requirements, with fines up to $50,000.

Directs the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to issue regulations outlining criteria for determining substantially AI-generated content and requirements for the disclosure statement. 

Requires the FEC to report on compliance and enforcement within two years and biannually thereafter to Congress.

Official name: Advancing Automation Research and Development in Agriculture Act
Casual name: Advancing Automation Research and Development in Agriculture Act
Short summary: Establishes a competitive grant program to advance mechanization and automation in specialty crops. Prioritizes projects enhancing labor efficiency, technology adoption, and human capital development. Requires non-Federal matching funds and scientific peer review. Allocates $20 million annually from 2024 onwards.
Long summary:
Establishes a stand-alone competitive research and extension grant program under the Department of Agriculture to advance mechanization and automation for specialty crops.

Mandates projects to create or improve cost-effective technologies that reduce manual labor and increase efficiency in crop production, resource management, and processing.

Requires projects to promote the adoption of mechanization and automation technologies by emphasizing factors such as connectivity, autonomy, and cost-effectiveness.

Specifies projects to accelerate automation through prototype development, in-field trials, and rapid commercialization.

Allows grants to be awarded to federal agencies, universities, research institutions, private organizations, and other eligible entities.

Mandates a competitive grant process based on scientific peer review and industry relevance.

Requires annual consultations with the specialty crops committee to review procedures, objectives, and grant awards.

Allocates $20 million annually from the Commodity Credit Corporation to fund the program starting fiscal year 2024.

Official name: International Artificial Intelligence Research Partnership Act of 2024
Casual name: International Artificial Intelligence Research Partnership Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes international AI research partnerships, encouraging U.S. cities to collaborate with international counterparts while aligning with U.S. research priorities. Prohibits partnerships with cities in countries of concern. Limits research to non-national security areas. Allocates $20 million annually for implementation.
Long summary:
Establishes international artificial intelligence research partnerships between U.S. cities and their international counterparts to promote AI leadership, economic cooperation, and workforce development.

Encourages public-private partnerships, including nonprofits and academic institutions, to support these AI research collaborations.

Requires coordination with the National Science Foundation to align activities with U.S. AI research priorities and leverage existing resources.

Prohibits partnerships with cities in countries deemed a national security concern or detrimental to U.S. foreign policy.

Limits research to non-national security AI areas and mandates adherence to export control regulations.

Authorizes the Secretary of State to enter contracts to facilitate partnerships and allocates $20 million annually from 2025 to 2028 for implementation.

Mandates annual reporting on partnerships, research scope, city participation, coordination efforts, and implementation challenges to relevant congressional committees.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Government support: AI workforce-related;Strategies: Government study or report

Official name: Expanding AI Voices Act
Casual name: Expanding AI Voices Act
Short summary: Amends the National AI Initiative Act to require the National Science Foundation to award grants to broaden AI research, education, and workforce development. Targets underrepresented institutions, fosters collaborations, and emphasizes ethical practices in AI education.
Long summary:
Amends the National AI Initiative Act by introducing a new subsection focused on expanding AI capacity.

Mandates the Director of the National Science Foundation to competitively award eligible higher education institutions and nonprofit organizations to enhance AI research, education, and workforce development.

Specifies eligibility for institutions not among the top 100 in federal R&D spending, historically Black colleges, minority-serving institutions, Tribal colleges, and their consortia.

Encourages collaborations with various stakeholders, including AI research institutes and private sector entities.

Allows use of funds for AI research programs, faculty development, resource access, community building, workshops, and ethical AI education.

Requires outreach to eligible entities, emphasizing underserved and underrepresented communities.

Ensures awarded projects complement existing programs without duplication.

Considers diversity, geographic distribution, and resource constraints in award decisions.

Official name: Workforce for AI Trust Act
Casual name: Workforce for AI Trust Act
Short summary: Amends the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act to support NSF fellowships for interdisciplinary AI research, emphasizing ethics and responsible practices. Enhances NIST's role in AI governance workforce development and risk management, focusing on education, technical standards, and professional certifications.
Long summary:
Amends the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020 to authorize the National Science Foundation (NSF) to support interdisciplinary AI fellowships, encouraging ethical AI practices.

Mandates the NSF to conduct outreach to diverse institutions and groups underrepresented in STEM fields for fellowship applicants.

Directs the NSF to promote AI-based training and workshops across STEM fields to accelerate scientific discovery and technology development.

Requires the integration of diverse disciplinary perspectives in AI research peer review panels.

Instructs the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to support education and workforce development to expand the AI governance workforce, focusing on managing AI-related risks.

Mandates NIST to develop technical standards and guidance for AI risk management careers, including mapping, measuring, and validating AI systems.

Official name: Oversee Emerging Technology Act
Casual name: Oversee Emerging Technology Act
Short summary: Requires federal agencies substantially engaged with emerging technologies to appoint a senior official as an emerging technology lead. Tasks them with advising on AI use, policy expertise, interagency collaboration, and procurement input. Mandates informing Congress of appointments and responsibilities.
Long summary:
Requires the head of each covered federal agency engaged in emerging technologies to appoint or designate a Senior Executive Service individual or equivalent as an emerging technology lead.

Mandates that these leads advise agencies on the responsible use of emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence.

Obliges these leads to provide expertise on responsible policies and practices, collaborate with interagency bodies, and input on procurement policies.

Instructs the President to inform Congress within 180 days of enactment about the appointed leads and their authorities and responsibilities.

Official name: To establish in the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration a program to improve precipitation forecasts, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H. R. 4913 NOAA Precipitation Forecasts
Short summary: Supports AI and machine learning research to enhance precipitation prediction accuracy and reliability. Encourages collaboration with academic and private sectors to evaluate emerging technologies. Uses high-performance computing to advance Earth System Models' predictive skills for extreme precipitation phenomena.
Long summary:
Establishes a program within the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to improve precipitation forecasts.

Requires the use of fully coupled Earth System Models to enhance precipitation prediction.

Mandates the utilization of high performance computing and other technologies, including machine learning and artificial intelligence, to advance precipitation forecasts.

Supports research and development in collaboration with academic and private sector partners to test and evaluate emerging technologies.

Improves the accuracy, reliability, and timeliness of precipitation predictions across various timescales.

Incorporates social and behavioral sciences to enhance precipitation forecast products and communication.

Coordinates efforts with Federal, State, local, Tribal, and academic entities.

Authorizes appropriations of $15 million annually from fiscal year 2024 through fiscal year 2028 for the program.

Official name: AI Grand Challenges Act of 2024
Casual name: AI Grand Challenges Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes the AI Grand Challenges Program to award competitive prizes for AI research in identified grand challenge areas like health, cybersecurity, and national security.
Long summary:
Establishes the AI Grand Challenges Program to stimulate AI research, development, and commercialization through prize competitions addressing grand challenges in various sectors like national security, health, and environment.

Requires the Director of the National Science Foundation, in coordination with public and private entities, to identify AI grand challenges and develop problem statements.

Mandates the creation of a specific grand challenge for AI-enabled cancer breakthroughs.

Sets eligibility criteria for participants, requiring private entities to be U.S.-based and individuals to be U.S. citizens or permanent residents.

Requires submission of a report within 60 days of awarding a prize, detailing the winning submission and its benefits, as well as biennial reports to Congress on program activities and public outreach, to be made publicly accessible.

Official name: To provide for Federal civilian agency laboratory development for testing and certification of artificial intelligence for civilian agency use, and for other purposes.
Casual name: Federal Civilian Agency AI Testing and Certification Act
Short summary: Requires Homeland Security, via FEMA, to develop AI testing centers for civilian agencies, ensuring rights protection and democratic principles. Establishes an AI Incident Reporting Office. Mandates biannual reports to Congress, and authorizes $20 billion for implementation.
Long summary:
Establishes federal civilian agency laboratories for AI testing and certification under the Secretary of Homeland Security through FEMA.

Mandates development of AI training and testing centers to evaluate AI systems for federal use, prioritizing privacy, transparency, accountability, and protection of individual rights.

Prohibits automated decision-making that threatens due process and constitutional rights.

Requires real-world use cases to inform AI system outcomes in federal settings, ensuring no personal data is used.

Creates a digital repository for use cases, ensuring Federal agencies can train and test AI systems appropriately.

Establishes an Office of Artificial Intelligence Incident Reporting within the Department of Homeland Security for agency collaboration and reporting on AI experiences.

Requires biannual reports from DHS to Congress on implementation, challenges, and resource needs, and agency reports on adverse AI experiences.

Authorizes $20 billion in appropriations to implement these measures.

Official name: Securing Growth and Robust Leadership in American Aviation Act, Sec. 908 ("Part 107 Waiver Improvements")
Casual name: American Aviation Act, Sec. 908 ("Part 107 Waiver Improvements")
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to leverage big data analytics and machine learning in reviewing requests for certificates of waiver under section 107.200 of title 14, Code of Federal Regulations.Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to improve waiver request processes by leveraging big data analytics and machine learning.Adopts a performance- and risk-based approach for waiver requests. Prohibits open-ended prompts unless necessary. Utilizes big data and machine learning for waiver data. Publishes waivers online, protecting proprietary info. Expedites similar waiver modifications and renewals. Considers property access control.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to leverage big data analytics and machine learning in reviewing requests for certificates of waiver under section 107.200 of title 14, Code of Federal Regulations.Adopts a performance- and risk-based approach for reviewing waiver requests under section 107.200 of title 14, CFR.

Improves the waiver application process and prohibits the use of open-ended descriptive prompts, except for unusual or irregular operations.

Leverages data, including big data analytics and machine learning, from previous waiver requests.

Requires consideration of control over access to real property within the operation area and recognizes safety enhancements from such control.

Publishes all issued certificates of waiver on the FAA website, including terms, conditions, limitations, and airspace class, while protecting proprietary information.

Allows streamlined approval for waiver applications substantially similar to previously approved ones.

Establishes an expedited review process for modifying or renewing previously issued waivers.

Official name: To direct the Secretary of Defense to establish a working group to develop and coordinate an artificial intelligence initiative among the Five Eyes countries, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H. R. 6425 (Five Eyes countries AI initiative)
Short summary: Directs the Secretary of Defense to establish the Five AIs Strategic AI Working Group to develop and coordinate AI initiatives among Five Eyes countries. Requires comparison of AI systems, testing interoperability, ethical frameworks, and expanding innovation efforts.
Long summary:
Directs the Secretary of Defense to establish the Five AIs Strategic Artificial Intelligence Working Group to develop and coordinate AI initiatives among Five Eyes countries. 

Outlines the organization of the working group, designating a senior civilian or military officer as head, and encouraging participation of Five Eyes Intelligence Oversight and Review Council members. 

Tasks the working group with comparing AI systems, identifying interoperability solutions, and testing AI model redundancy.

Instructs the development of shared strategies for AI research, development, and data management.

Requires the submission of a plan for the establishment and activities of the working group within 60 days and biannual reports to congressional committees.

Official name: Protecting Kids on Social Media Act
Casual name: Protecting Kids on Social Media Act
Short summary: Prohibits social media platforms from using algorithmic recommendation systems for users under 18, and bans access of users under 13. Requires age verification and parental consent for minors on social media, and establishes enforcement mechanisms and a secure digital identification credential pilot program.
Long summary:
Requires social media platforms to verify the age of users beyond simple attestations, using current age verification technologies.

Prohibits social media platforms from allowing individuals under 13 from accessing their services.

Mandates parental or guardian consent for users under 18, requiring platforms to take reasonable steps to verify this consent.

Prohibits the use of algorithmic recommendation systems on individuals under 18, except for contextual suggestions or advertising.

Establishes a Pilot Program for secure digital identification credentials to facilitate age and parental consent verification, overseen by the Secretary of Commerce.

Empowers the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general to enforce compliance, with civil penalties for violations.

Grants extraterritorial jurisdiction for violations involving individuals in the United States.

Official name: Transparent Automated Governance Act
Casual name: TAG Act
Short summary: Requires the Director to issue guidance on automated systems for critical decisions, including public notice and appeals processes. Obligates agencies to implement the guidance and mandates the Comptroller General to review compliance.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to issue guidance for transparent automated governance, ensuring compliance with privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties protections.

Mandates agencies to disclose and allow appeals when utilizing automated systems for critical decisions affecting individuals’ rights or status.

Instructs agencies to design systems providing plain language notifications and establish appeals processes for decisions made using AI-augmented processes.

Obligates agencies to track the accuracy and reliability of AI systems, and share data with relevant authorities; encourages consultation with governmental and private sectors for guidance development.

Requires the Comptroller General to report on agency compliance, with reports submitted biennially.

Short summary: Amends Section 401 of the National Quantum Initiative Act to require the Department of Energy to conduct quantum information science research and promote commercialization. Requires strategic plans for high-performance computing, cooperation with industry, and development of relevant supply chains. Prohibits funding partnerships with entities posing national security threats.
Long summary:
Amends the National Quantum Initiative Act to mandate the Secretary of Energy to conduct a program focusing on quantum information science research, development, and commercialization.

Operates National Quantum Information Science Research Centers to facilitate breakthroughs in quantum technologies.

Requires collaboration with industry, National Laboratories, and educational institutions for technology development.

Engages with the quantum technology industry to promote the commercialization of applications relevant to the Department of Energy's activities.

Submits a 10-year strategic plan to integrate quantum and AI with high-performance computing systems.

Establishes an early-stage quantum high-performance computing research program.

Conducts a study on the quantum supply chain to identify growth obstacles and recommends strategies to bolster domestic manufacturing and workforce development.

Launches a university-led traineeship program to develop the quantum workforce.

Official name: Candidate Voice Fraud Prohibition Act
Casual name: Candidate Voice Fraud Prohibition Act
Short summary: Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act to prohibit distributing AI-generated audio impersonating candidates' voices with intent to mislead voters, exempting certain media entities and satire. Imposes fines or imprisonment for violations, and requires the Federal Election Commission to issue regulations and reports on compliance.
Long summary:
Prohibits the distribution of political communications containing materially deceptive AI-generated audio that impersonates a candidate’s voice, with actual malice, intended to harm the candidate’s reputation or deceive voters.

Applies to communications distributed within 90 days of a general election or 60 days of a primary, special, or runoff election.

Exempts certain entities from liability such as radio or television stations that air deceptive AI-generated content as part of news coverage or communications that clearly constitute satire.

Defines "materially deceptive audio" as AI-generated impersonation of a candidate’s voice without approval and lacking a clear disclaimer.

Imposes criminal penalties of fines or up to 2 years imprisonment for individuals who knowingly and willfully violate the prohibition.

Requires the Federal Election Commission (FEC) to issue regulations to clarify disclaimer requirements and mandates the submission of annual compliance reports to Congress.

Official name: Integrating New Technologies to Empower Law Enforcement at Our Borders Act
Casual name: New Technologies at Borders Act
Short summary: Requires the Department of Homeland Security to plan integrating AI and emerging technologies for border awareness. Specifies assessments, cost estimates, capability gaps, and coordination with other agencies. Emphasizes transitioning new technologies and incentivizing private sector development.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Homeland Security to develop a comprehensive plan within 180 days to identify, deploy, and integrate emerging and advanced technologies to enhance situational awareness along the northern and southern borders of the United States.

Mandates the inclusion of artificial intelligence, machine learning, automation, fiber-optic sensing, nanotechnology, optical and cognitive radar, modeling and simulation technology, hyperspectral and LIDAR sensors, and other similar technologies.

Includes an assessment of current technology utilization by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and how capability gaps can be addressed through technology integration.

Requires cost estimates for technology acquisition and maintenance, metrics for measuring technology impact, and an assessment of multi-domain awareness improvements.

Mandates an analysis of other Federal technologies, procurement authorities, and the transition of new technology systems.

Requires coordination with the Department of Homeland Security's Science and Technology Directorate for research, development, and incentivizing private sector innovation.

Defines Artificial Intelligence

Creates a program called ‘‘AI Center of Excellence’’, (CoE) which shall facilitate the adoption of AI technologies in the Federal Government and improve cohesion in the adoption of artificial intelligence.

Stipulates certain functions of this program which includes; 
a.  Collaborating with individual agencies, federal laboratories, nonprofit organizations, institutions and other entities to discuss recent developments in AI.

b. Assisting agencies in applying Federal policies on the management and use of data in the use of AI.

c. Consulting with agencies, including the Department of Defense, the Department of Commerce, the Department of Energy, the Department of Homeland Security, the Office of Management and Budget, the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, and the National Science Foundation, on issues relating to AI.

Identifies the skills needed for future job positions within the CoE and establishes jobs within the CoE and their duration (2 and 5 years).

Instructs the Director of the CoE to submit a comprehensive plan to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs and the Committee on Oversight and Reform with a timeline to achieve paragraphs a,b,c,d within 120 days of the date of the enactment of the instrument.

Instructs the Director of the AI CoE to recommend approaches to remove barriers for use by agencies of AI technologies in order to promote their innovative application while protecting civil rights, and economic and national security; identify best practices for assessing and mitigating any discriminatory impact or bias on the basis of any classification protected under Federal nondiscrimination laws, or any unintended consequence of the use of AI.

Official name: Identifying Outputs of Generative Adversarial Networks Act
Casual name: IOGAN Act
Short summary: Directs the Director of the National Science Foundation to support research on outputs generated by generative adversarial networks, such as deepfakes, and other comparable techniques.
Long summary:
Directs the Director of the National Science Foundation to support research on manipulated or synthesized content and information authenticity.

Directs the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology to support research for the development of measurements and standards on generative adversarial networks and other technologies that synthesize or manipulate content.

Requires the Directors to submit a report within a year to Congress of research findings and policy recommendations.

Official name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title III ("National Science Foundation for the Future")
Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title III ("National Science Foundation for the Future")
Short summary: Provides scholarships and fellowships for AI-related studies to recruit and train the next generation of AI professionals in US government. Grants competitive grants and fellowships for organizations advancing risk assessment through AI. Adds AI and machine learning to the list of research areas that the Director of the National Science Foundation can award grants to. Makes awards available for supporting basic research to advance critical minerals mining strategies that involve AI. Requires the development and testing of models to inform implementation of government-wide data infrastructure for statistical activities. Requires a study on U.S. higher education's AI research capacity and an annual update to key technology focus areas.
Long summary:
Instructs the Director of the National Science Foundation (Director) to develop a report on the need and feasibility as well as construct plans to implement a program to recruit and train the next generation of AI professionals in US government. Mandates that the report include statistical data on the federal AI workforce, assessments of current and future demand for AI expertise across the federal government and the ability of higher education institutions to prepare students to meet this demand, and an evaluation of the need for and feasibility of establishing a scholarship-for-service program for AI professionals in US government, as well as the possibility of leveraging existing programs such as the Federal Cyber Scholarship-for-Service Program. 

Authorizes the Director and other appropriate agency heads to establish a Federal AI scholarship-for-service program to recruit and train AI professionals to achieve the missions of US government. Requires the Director, in coordination with other agency heads, to establish criteria such as excellence in AI education and the ability to attract diverse students to designate qualified institutions of higher education that shall be eligible to participate in the program. Requires the program to provide scholarships through qualified higher education institutions to students pursuing fields of study related to AI, provide scholarship recipients with temporary appointments related to work on AI in the federal government, prioritize placement of scholarship recipients in executive agencies, identify opportunities to promote multidisciplinary programs of study, support capacity building for educational institutions to enable them to train the next-generation AI workforce, create courses in technology ethics for scholarship recipients, and award fellowships to masters and doctoral students who are pursuing degrees or research in AI and related fields. Instructs scholarship amounts to cover a student’s tuition and fees for not more than 3 years with an additional stipend, and obligates scholarship recipients to work for a period of time equivalent to the length of the scholarship in the AI mission of the US government entity or affiliated entity.

Instructs the Director to establish competitive awards to institutions to advance knowledge of risk assessment including the utilization of AI in our ability to understand and predict extreme events and natural hazards, create engineered system solutions and develop instrumentation for innovation in resilient infrastructure, and advance research on multidisciplinary wildfire science and behaviors related to increasing resilience.

Amends Section 506(a) of the America COMPETES Reauthorization Act of 2010 by adding AI and machine learning to the list of research areas that fall under information technologies for manufacturing that the Director can award grants to.

Instructs the Director to make competitive awards to institutions to support basic research to advance critical minerals mining strategies to make better use of domestic resources and eliminate national reliance on minerals subject to supply chain disruptions, including the application of AI for geological exploration of critical minerals and the application of machine learning for detection and sorting of critical minerals.

Requires the Director to conduct or support the development of a study on AI research capacity at US institutions of higher education. Requires the study to, at minimum, address which universities are putting out significant peer-reviewed AI research based on quantity and number of citations, which factors of these universities such as computing power enable their research, promising practices at these universities for advancing diversity, equity, and inclusion in AI research programs, geographic diversity of universities that have AI research-enabling factors, and how universities that are not currently top producers of AI research could improve, including potential pilot programs that the federal government could develop or support to help universities produce AI research.

Instructs the Director, in consultation with the Director of the Office of Management and Budget and the interagency committee established under the National AI Initiative Act of 2020, to develop and test models to inform the implementation of a government-wide data linkage and access infrastructure for statistical activities.

Requires the Director to identify, and annually review and update a list of not more than 10 key technology focus areas, which includes AI, machine learning, autonomy, and related advances.

Official name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title II ("National Institute of Standards and Technology for the Future"), Subtitle B ("Measurement Research")
Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title II ("National Institute of Standards and Technology for the Future"), Subtitle B ("Measurement Research")
Short summary: Enhances Artificial Intelligence Systems through research and testing, optimizing data sets, and evaluating network architectures. Necessitates measurement research for advanced communications. Supports AI and data science development. Requires a risk management tech framework and publishing cybersecurity tools for trustworthy AI systems. Mandates cover creating testbeds for robust system development and conducting regular testing to improve biometric identification technologies.
Long summary:
Instructs the Director to carry out research and testing to improve the effectiveness of Artificial Intelligence-enabled cybersecurity, Artificial Intelligence defense systems and evaluating the performance of varying network architectures at strengthening network security.

Requires the Director to carry out a program of measurement research for advanced communications technologies which among others would include Artificial Intelligence systems to enable internet of things networks, immersive technology, and other advanced communications technologies

Instructs the Director to support the development of technical standards and guidelines that promote safe and trustworthy Artificial Intelligence systems, such as enhancing the accuracy, privacy, explanability, reliability, robustness, safety, security, and mitigation of harmful bias in Artificial Intelligence systems

Instructs the Director to create a framework for managing risks associated with Artificial Intelligence systems.

Instructs the Director to develop and publish cybersecurity tools, encryption methods, and best practices for Artificial Intelligence and data science. 

Requires the Director to establish testbeds to support the development of robust and trustworthy Artificial Intelligence and machine learning systems, including testbeds that examine the vulnerabilities and conditions that may lead to failure in, malfunction of, or attacks on such systems.

Requires the Director to conduct regular testing to improve and benchmark the accuracy, efficacy, and bias of biometric identification technologies,

Official name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title I ("Department of Energy Science for the Future")
Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title I ("Department of Energy Science for the Future")
Short summary: Initiates several projects using artificial intelligence and emerging technologies, all directives emphasizing support for artificial intelligence and data science development, establishing technical standards, and creating testbeds for robust systems. Efforts on scholarships, fellowships, and ongoing research to nurture the next generation of artificial intelligence professionals and enhance biometric identification technologies.
Long summary:
Instructs the Secretary of the Advanced Computing Program to maintain foundational research programs in mathematical and computational sciences, including new and emerging computing needs within the mission of the Department like novel approaches to modelling and simulation, artificial intelligence, and machine learning.

Instructs the Secretary to issue and ensure adherence to guidance for the Department, the National Laboratories, and users. This guidance pertains to how high-performance computing systems, including machine learning algorithms for data analysis and artificial intelligence, should be employed. 

Instructed the Director to prioritize research and development of sustainable chemistry, autonomous chemistry, and materials synthesis, leveraging  advances in artificial intelligence.

Instructs the Director to carry out research and testing to improve the effectiveness of artificial intelligence-enabled cybersecurity. 

Instructs the Director to support the development of artificial intelligence and data science. 

Instructs the Director to support the development of technical standards and guidelines- creating a framework for managing risks associated with artificial intelligence systems. 

Instructs the Director to establish testbeds to support the development of robust and trustworthy artificial intelligence and machine learning systems. These testbeds will examine the vulnerabilities and conditions that may lead to failure, malfunction, or attacks on such systems.

Instructs the Director to establish a report on the need, feasibility, and plans to implement a program to recruit and train the next generation of artificial intelligence professionals. This program is meant to meet the needs of Federal, State, local, and Tribal governments.

Provides scholarships through qualified institutions of higher education to students enrolled in programs related to artificial intelligence. Provides fellowships to masters and doctoral students pursuing degrees or research in artificial intelligence and related fields.

Necessitates research and testing to improve the accuracy, efficacy, and bias of biometric identification technologies.

Instructs the Director of the Office of Science to establish 4 Microelectronic Science Research Centers, whose missions include advancing knowledge and experimental capabilities in AI and other computational and theoretical methods.

Promotes coordination of microelectronic research and development across DOE efforts, other Federal agencies, other research centers, and the private sector.

Casual name: Research and Development, Competition, and Innovation Act, Title VI, Section 10771 ("Department of Energy research, development, and demonstration activities")
Short summary: Authorizes the appropriation of $200 million to the U.S. Department of Energy to carry out environmental management research, development, and demonstration activities, including those relating to artificial intelligence.
Long summary: Authorizes the appropriation of $200 million -- spread across FY 2023-2026 -- to the U.S. Department of Energy for its Office of Environmental Management to carry out research, development, and demonstration activities, including those relating to artificial intelligence.
