Official name: Literacy in Future Technologies Artificial Intelligence Act
Casual name: LIFT AI Act
Short summary: Establishes awards for developing K-12 AI literacy curricula. Supports research and professional development to enhance AI literacy. Defines AI literacy as the ability to use and interpret AI effectively and ethically.
Long summary:
Establishes the "LIFT AI Act" to advance AI literacy in K–12 education.

Empowers the Director of the National Science Foundation to award competitive grants to higher education institutions or nonprofits for developing AI literacy curricula and evaluation methods.

Endorses AI literacy education to prepare a skilled workforce and maintain U.S. leadership in AI.

Recognizes the importance of AI literacy in mitigating ethical challenges and adapting to rapidly evolving AI technologies.

Supports the development of curricula focussed on AI competency, AI literacy evaluation tools, and professional development courses in AI literacy for educators.

Encourages incorporating AI literacy in existing curricula, emphasizing responsible AI use.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Acquisitions Act of 2024
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Acquisitions Act of 2024
Short summary: Prohibits federal agencies from acquiring AI products/services linked to foreign adversaries, requiring divestment within specified timelines. Mandates public disclosure of risky AI products/services. Exempts intelligence and specific Department of Defense activities from these restrictions.
Long summary:
Mandates the development of a list of artificial intelligence products or services posing national security risks, focusing on those associated with foreign entities of concern.

Requires the Executive Director, alongside the Under Secretary of Commerce and the Federal Acquisition Security Council, to publish and annually update this list.

Prohibits federal agencies from purchasing or procuring AI products or services included on this list.

Obligates contractors with federal agencies to divest from listed AI products within specified timeframes.

Allows exceptions for intelligence activities and specific Department of Defense operations related to defense intelligence and adversarial AI research.

Defines key terms including "artificial intelligence," "foreign person of concern," and "country of concern," specifying entities like China, Russia, and Iran as countries of concern.

Sets a timeline for initial list publication within one year of the Act's enactment and mandates public notification if no updates are made annually.

Official name: A bill to specify control and management of Department of Defense data and to establish the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer Governing Council, and for other purposes.
Casual name: S. 3004 (Chief AI Officer Governing Council)
Short summary: Establishes the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer Governing Council to oversee AI policy, ethics, and coordination within the Department of Defense. Requires the Council to streamline AI development, ensure ethical practices, and provide periodic reports to Congress.
Long summary:
Establishes the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Officer Governing Council to oversee and ensure ethical AI and data policies within the Department of Defense.

Requires the Council to oversee AI ethics, governance, and operational usage, with the aim of improving AI governance coordination and mitigating bias in AI systems. 

Mandates the Council to submit reports to the Secretary of Defense and congressional defense committees every 180 days initially and at least every 18 months thereafter.

Official name: Digital Platform Commission Act of 2023
Casual name: Digital Platform Commission Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes the Federal Digital Platform Commission to regulate digital platforms, including AI processes, ensuring fairness, transparency, and safety. Provides for the creation of a Code Council to develop voluntary or enforceable policy related to digital platforms and AI processes.
Long summary:
Defines algorithmic processes as including artificial intelligence techniques and refers to processes that can make decisions, generate content, or provide information, especially as used in social media

Establishes the Federal Digital Platform Commission to regulate digital platforms, including social media.

Provides for the creation of a Code Council to develop voluntary or enforceable behavioral codes, technical standards, and other policies related to digital platforms and AI processes.

Mandates oversight of algorithmic processes, including those using AI, for fairness, transparency, and safety.

Obligates platforms to ensure recommendation systems and other algorithmic processes are free from harmful, abusive, anticompetitive, or deceptive biases.

Imposes regular public risk assessments for harmful content distribution and steps to mitigate such harms, including those from algorithmic processes..

Official name: Cooper Davis Act
Casual name: Cooper Davis Act
Short summary: Amends the Controlled Substances Act to require electronic communication and remote computing service providers to report suspected illegal activities involving controlled substances to the DEA. Permits algorithmic content moderation, imposes penalties for noncompliance, and mandates annual reporting by the DEA.
Long summary:
Requires electronic communication service providers and remote computing services to report to the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) any knowledge of crimes involving fentanyl, methamphetamine, or counterfeit substances.

Mandates the report to be filed within 60 days of obtaining such knowledge, including detailed contact and relevant information about the involved accounts.

Allows reports based on reasonable belief and includes optional additional information like geographic location and complete communication data.

Subjects providers to criminal and civil penalties for failing to report or submitting false reports.

States that providers are not required to monitor users or the content of communications proactively.

Obliges DEA to review and potentially investigate reports, sharing them with other law enforcement agencies as needed.

Mandates the Attorney General to enforce these requirements and take measures for data minimization.

Requires DEA to publish annual reports on the number and outcomes of reports received.

Exempts broadband internet access service providers from these reporting requirements.
Tags: Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: About incidents

Official name: AI Ads Act
Casual name: AI Ads Act
Short summary: Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act to include AI-generated content in prohibitions against fraudulent misrepresentation and solicitation. Expands the scope to cover any person misrepresenting candidates, parties, or committees for any purpose, not just candidates themselves.
Long summary:
Amends the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 to include fraudulent misrepresentation through content generated with artificial intelligence (AI) under its prohibitions.

Clarifies that fraudulent misrepresentation of campaign authority or solicitation of funds includes the use of AI-generated content.

Extends the prohibition against fraudulent misrepresentation beyond candidates to any person misrepresenting candidates, political parties, or committees for any purpose.

Broadens the scope of prohibited fraudulent solicitation to encompass any candidate, political party, or fictitious political organization, not just those directly involving a candidate or party employee.

Expands the provisions to cover AI-generated content used in misrepresentation or solicitation within political campaigns, ensuring comprehensive regulation of AI's role in electoral fraud.

Official name: S.4306, Five AIs Act 2024
Casual name: Five AIs Act 2024
Short summary: Establishes the "Five AIs Strategic Artificial Intelligence Working Group" to coordinate AI initiatives among Five Eyes countries. Tasks include enhancing AI interoperability, testing systems, managing data, leveraging commercial AI technologies, and sharing results with allies. Controls export of technical data.
Long summary:
Establishes the "Five AIs Strategic Artificial Intelligence Working Group" within 90 days to coordinate AI initiatives among the Five Eyes countries (Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the UK, and the US).

Requires the Secretary of Defense and Director of National Intelligence to lead and designate a head for the Working Group.

Mandates the Working Group to develop strategies for AI interoperability, ethical frameworks, and innovation among the Five Eyes countries.

Instructs testing and evaluation of AI systems for intelligence sharing and battlespace awareness.

Emphasizes leveraging commercial AI technologies and managing data-informed AI systems.

Requires submission of a plan within 60 days and biannual reports on the Working Group's activities.

Specifies control of knowledge and technical data by individual countries under their export laws.

Allows termination of the Working Group on September 30, 2028, with possible extension for national security interests.

Official name: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency Securing AI Task Force Act
Casual name: CISA Securing AI Task Force Act
Short summary: Establishes a CISA Task Force to coordinate AI safety and security efforts, assess initiatives, address workforce gaps, and promote secure AI adoption. Requires biannual briefings to Congress. Ceases effect five years post-enactment.
Long summary:
Establishes a Task Force within the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency to coordinate efforts related to AI safety and security.

Requires coordination of Agency efforts to improve the safe and secure design, development, adoption, and deployment of AI.

Ensures Agency collaboration in interagency and nongovernment AI-related initiatives.

Mandates assessment and evaluation of Agency safety initiatives to address AI challenges.

Leverages expertise to use AI in defending against cyber threats.

Coordinates efforts to advise stakeholders on AI-related cyber risks.

Identifies AI workforce gaps and proposes solutions.

Promotes secure adoption of AI-based software.

Recommends new initiatives for AI safety and security.

Ensures AI efforts meet privacy, civil rights, and civil liberties standards.

Supports publication and accuracy of the Agency’s AI use inventory.

Mandates biannual briefings to Congress on Task Force activities.

Sunsets the Act 5 years after enactment.

Official name: No AI Audits Act
Casual name: No AI Audits Act
Short summary: Limits IRS use of AI for audits by requiring guidance as rule-making and adherence to NIST explainability principles. Requires Comptroller General to audit and report on IRS's AI use. Mandates that only IRS staff can initiate taxpayer investigations.
Long summary:
Limits the Internal Revenue Service's (IRS) use of artificial intelligence (AI) for audits or investigations.

Requires any IRS guidance on AI for audits to follow rule-making procedures under Section 553 of Title 5, U.S. Code.

Prohibits the IRS from conducting audits or investigations initiated by AI unless the AI meets explainability principles set by the National Institute of Standards and Technology.

Defines "artificial intelligence" according to the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020.

Mandates the Comptroller General to audit and report on the IRS's AI use, including a cost-benefit analysis and the impact on taxpayer rights.

Stipulates that IRS investigations or examinations must be initiated by a staff investigator, with documentation including the investigator's contact information.
Tags: Risk factors: Interpretability and explainability;Applications: Government: other applications/unspecified;Strategies: Performance requirements;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Disclosure;Risk factors: Transparency;Applications: Government: other applications/unspecified;Applications: Government: other applications/unspecified

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Weapon Accountability and Risk Evaluation Act of 2024
Casual name: AWARE Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to create a ledger of AI-enabled military systems with regular updates and requires a comprehensive risk assessment process covering dependability, cybersecurity, privacy, and bias. Requires annual assessments, annotations for exports, and public reporting to ensure transparency and accountability.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Defense to create a ledger of all uses by the Department of Defense of AI-enabled weapon, targeting, and decision support systems within three years.

Requires the ledger to be a structured, indexed database that is regularly updated.

Calls on the Secretary of Defense to develop a risk assessment process to evaluate dependability, cybersecurity, privacy, and civilian harm risk for each AI system.

Mandates annual reevaluations of each system, and when an AI model receives an update, a new system is procured, or a new weapons review is conducted.

Requires annotations in the ledger when AI systems are shared, exported, or used internationally, noting additional risks.

Obligates annual progress reports and submissions to Congress, including the ledger and risk assessment findings, to be publicly accessible

Official name: Simplify, Don't Amplify the IRS Act, Sec. 201 ("Tax Gap Projection")
Casual name: Simplify IRS Act, Sec. 201 ("Tax Gap Projection")
Short summary: Requires the Commissioner of Internal Revenue to use AI, including neural machine learning, to calculate tax gap projections, reducing the burden on taxpayers. Limits National Research Program audits to fiscal year 2022 levels while leveraging commercial data analysis tools.
Long summary:
Mandates the Commissioner of Internal Revenue to use artificial intelligence, including neural machine learning, to reduce taxpayer burden during National Research Program audits and improve the accuracy of tax gap projections.

Requires use of AI and data analysis tools to calculate and project the tax gap, which is the difference between taxes owed and taxes collected.

Prohibits conducting more National Research Program audits in any fiscal year than the number conducted in fiscal year 2022. 

Obligates the Commissioner to submit an annual tax gap projection to Congress, detailing the estimate using the latest data available, by July 31 each year.

Defines the term "tax gap" as the difference between tax liabilities owed under the Internal Revenue Code and those actually collected by the IRS.

Official name: DEEPFAKES Accountability Act, Sec. 2 ("Transparency Requirements")
Casual name: DEEPFAKES Accountability Act, Sec. 2 ("Transparency Requirements")
Short summary: Amends U.S. Code to require disclosures and inclusion of content provenance technologies for AI-generated false personation records, with specific disclosure requirements based on the form of the record, and imposes criminal and civil penalties for non-compliance.
Long summary:
Outlines transparency requirements for advanced technological false personation records, requiring producers of such records to include content provenance technologies to identify alterations, as well as disclosures to detail the extent of alterations.

Mandates specific disclosure formats for audiovisual, visual, and audio records.

Imposes criminal penalties, including fines and imprisonment of up to 5 years, for intentionally failing to disclose or altering existing disclosures.

Imposes civil penalties up to $150,000 per violation and allows for private rights of action for damages and injunctive relief.

Establishes coordinators for deepfake-related violations in each U.S. Attorney’s Office.

Official name: Sensible Classification Act of 2023
Casual name: Sensible Classification Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Office of Electronic Government, in consultation with key defense, intelligence and declassification officials, to research and report on AI and machine learning solutions for efficient classification and declassification.
Long summary:
Mandates the research and recommendation of AI and machine learning solutions for efficient classification and declassification systems, to be interoperable across the Federal Government.

Requires the Administrator of the Office of Electronic Government to consult with key defense and intelligence agencies for technology implementation and submit a report on the recommended solution.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Government study or report;Applications: Government: other applications/unspecified

Official name: Federal AI Governance and Transparency Act
Casual name: Federal AI Governance and Transparency Act
Short summary: Establishes governance requirements for Federal AI systems to ensure actions align with laws, promote fairness, manage risks, and maintain transparency. Requires the development of AI charters and oversight, with provisions for public disclosure, privacy protection, and periodic evaluations.
Long summary:
Amends chapter 35 of title 44, United States Code, to establish Federal AI system governance requirements.

Instructs Federal actions involving AI to align with the Constitution, relevant laws, and policies, protecting civil rights and privacy.

Mandates the assessment and management of AI risks before deployment, ensuring AI outcomes remain ethical and accurate.

Requires transparency and explainability of AI systems' purpose, operations, and outcomes to maintain safety and security, and protect against vulnerabilities.

Obligates regular testing and oversight of AI applications to ensure they align with intended purposes and create mechanisms to deactivate AI systems that do not meet standards.

Instructs agencies to publicly disclose AI use to stakeholders and establish notification and appeal processes for AI-based decisions.

Establishes a Federal AI System Inventory for cataloging agency AI governance charters to be publicly available and regularly updated.

Official name: To direct the Director of the National Institutes of Health to establish a grant program to facilitate research regarding the use of generative artificial intelligence in health care, and for other purposes.
Casual name: H.R. 7381 (Health AI)
Short summary: Directs the NIH Director to establish a grant program promoting research on generative AI in healthcare. Prioritizes projects enhancing AI adoption, workforce development, reducing disparities, and improving underserved care. Supports AI applications in documentation, insurance, and customer service efficiency.
Long summary:
Establishes a grant program by the Director of the National Institutes of Health to fund research on the use of generative AI in health care.

Permits research to include enhancing note-taking, reducing administrative burdens, expediting insurance claims, improving customer service efficiency, and other health care improvements.

Prioritizes grants for entities promoting AI adoption in health care, investing in workforce development, mitigating workforce burnout, reducing health disparities, and improving care for medically underserved populations.

Defines eligible entities to include higher education institutions, nonprofit organizations, and various governmental agencies.

Defines "generative artificial intelligence" as AI producing content in response to prompts.

Official name: STAND with Taiwan Act of 2023
Casual name: STAND with Taiwan Act
Short summary: Imposes sanctions on China if its military invades Taiwan, including blocking property, prohibiting trades, and investment restrictions. Targets Chinese officials, institutions, and entities involved in aggressive actions. Coordinates with allies for sanctions, allowing exceptions for national security or intelligence activities.
Long summary:
Mandates the imposition of sanctions on the People's Republic of China if the People’s Liberation Army initiates a military invasion of Taiwan. 

Requires sanctions against Chinese officials and entities involved in military campaigns or efforts undermining Taiwan's security and infrastructure.

Blocks property and imposes visa restrictions on identified Chinese officials and affiliates involved in military and coercive actions against Taiwan.

Prohibits U.S. financial institutions from investments that benefit Chinese government-controlled entities or priority sectors like artificial intelligence and other advanced technologies.

Bans the listing and trading of securities of Chinese entities on U.S. securities exchanges following an invasion.

Prohibits the importation of goods from China post-invasion unless exceptions for national security are made.

Grants the President authority to enforce sanctions and provides for penalties under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Bug Bounty Act of 2023
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Bug Bounty Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the Department of Defense to develop a bug bounty program for AI products within 180 days. Directs briefing to Congress on program development and future plans within one year.
Long summary:
Mandates the Chief Data and Artificial Intelligence Officer of the Department of Defense to develop a bug bounty program for AI products within 180 days of enactment.

Allows collaboration with other government agencies possessing cybersecurity and AI expertise during program development.

Authorizes the implementation of the developed bug bounty program.

Ensures that Department of Defense contracts permit participation in the bug bounty program.

Requires the Chief to brief congressional defense committees within one year on the development, implementation, and long-term plans of relevant bug bounty programs.
Tags: Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Risk factors: Reliability;Strategies: Evaluation: Adversarial testing;Risk factors: Reliability: Robustness;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: Disclosure;Strategies: Disclosure: About evaluation

Official name: AI Labeling Act of 2023
Casual name: AI Labeling Act
Short summary: Requires AI-generated content to include clear and conspicuous disclosures on AI-generated content in image, video, audio, multimedia, and text, and mandates developers and licensees to prevent disclosure removal; establishes a working group to create AI-content detection standards, with enforcement by the Federal Trade Commission.
Long summary:
Requires content generated by AI to include clear and conspicuous disclosures marking content as AI-generated. 

Mandates the disclosure to identify content as AI-generated, the tool used to create output, and the creation date and time, ensuring disclosures are permanent or not easily removed.

Compels developers and third-party licensees of generative AI systems to implement procedures preventing content distribution without disclosures, including contractual obligations and certifying compliance.

Treats violations as unfair or deceptive acts under the Federal Trade Commission Act, granting enforcement powers to the FTC.

Establishes a working group to develop AI-generated content detection standards and assist platforms in identifying AI-generated content.

Requires the working group to publish a framework with guidelines and submit a report to Congress recommending legislative or administrative actions.

Official name: DOE and USDA Interagency Research Act
Casual name: DOE and USDA Interagency Research Act
Short summary: Requires DOE and USDA to conduct collaborative AI research for agriculture and energy. Establishes interagency agreements for competitive, merit-reviewed processes and supports data sharing, grid security, and technology development. Mandates a report on coordination and achievements.
Long summary:
Mandates the Department of Energy and Department of Agriculture to engage in joint research and development activities.

Requires a memorandum of understanding to facilitate these activities through a competitive, merit-reviewed process.

Allows collaborative research in areas including artificial intelligence, machine learning, and modeling for optimizing agriculture and energy algorithms.

Promotes data integration, sharing, and secure access aligned with Federal regulations.

Encourages workforce development and infrastructure support as deemed necessary by the Secretaries.

Authorizes reimbursable agreements and collaboration with other Federal agencies to enhance research effectiveness.

Obliges the Secretaries to report on interagency coordination, research achievements, and future opportunities within two years to relevant Congressional Committees.

Ensures activities comply with research security mandates under relevant U.S. Code.

Official name: Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Research and Control Amendments Act of 2023, Sec. 2f ("National Harmful Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Observing Network")
Casual name: Algal Bloom Research and Control Act, Sec. 2f ("Algal Bloom and Hypoxia Observing Network")
Short summary: Establishes a national network of harmful algal bloom observing systems using AI for detection and forecasting. Requires data integration and dissemination through a Harmful Algal Bloom Data Assembly Center. Coordinates between NCCOS and IOOS Program Office for effective management.
Long summary:
Amends the Harmful Algal Blooms and Hypoxia Research and Control Act of 1998 to establish a national harmful algal bloom observing network.

Requires the Under Secretary, through NCCOS and IOOS of NOAA, to monitor, detect, and forecast harmful algal blooms using emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence.

Mandates the IOOS Program Office to coordinate with NCCOS on data integration and information dissemination.

Establishes a Harmful Algal Bloom Data Assembly Center for data integration, dissemination, and support of ecological forecasting.

Official name: Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act of 2023, Sec. 401 ("Emerging Technology")
Casual name: Communications, Video, and Technology Accessibility Act, Sec. 401 ("Emerging Technology")
Short summary: Requires the Commission to report on accessibility barriers in emerging AI and communication technologies. Mandates consideration of specific disabilities' impact. Obliges regulations update every 5 years to ensure accessible AI technologies for disabled individuals.
Long summary:
Amends Title VII of the Communications Act of 1934 to enhance accessibility.

Requires the Commission, in consultation with the U.S. Access Board, to submit reports to Congress every 5 years.

Assesses accessibility barriers for individuals with disabilities in emerging technologies, including those using AI, augmented reality, and more.

Identifies needed solutions to ensure accessibility and compatibility with peripheral devices for individuals with disabilities.

Mandates consideration of effects on individuals with specific barriers and diverse backgrounds.

Obliges the Commission to issue new or update existing regulations within 2 years after each report to ensure accessibility of emerging technologies.

Focuses on technologies like AI and advanced computing, impacting disability accessibility policies.

Official name: Americas Act, Title II ("Trade and Investment for the Americas"), Sec. 202 ("Americas Partnership Business Advisory Board")
Casual name: Americas Act, Title II ("Trade and Investment"), Sec. 202 ("Business Advisory Board")
Short summary: Advises Americas partner countries on aligning regulations related to emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence. Coordinates with USMCA central regulatory bodies. Requires an annual report on business environments, highlighting investment opportunities and challenges.
Long summary:
Establishes a business advisory board under the Americas Partnership Secretariat to meet periodically and provide guidance.

Composes the board of representatives from private sector entities, civil society, and labor organizations from partner countries.

Allows the board to advise on issues including regulatory hurdles, labor issues, dispute resolution, legal investment hurdles, and alignment on regulations related to emerging technologies like artificial intelligence.

Requires coordination with central regulatory bodies as outlined in the USMCA.

Mandates the board to submit an annual report on the business environment, highlighting opportunities and challenges to investment in partner countries.

Official name: Targeting Online Sales of Fentanyl Act
Casual name: Targeting Online Sales of Fentanyl Act
Short summary: Requires a GAO study on AI's role in detecting and combating illicit online fentanyl and methamphetamine sales, focusing on algorithmic technologies, enforcement mechanisms, and interagency collaborations. Mandates a report to Congress within one year detailing findings.
Long summary:
Mandates the Comptroller General to conduct a study on online sales of fentanyl and methamphetamine, analyzing business models, supply chains, and logistics.

Requires examination of the impact on individuals under 18 and federal efforts to combat these sales, including interagency and intergovernmental collaborations.

Analyzes existing federal law enforcement procedures and outcomes over the past 10 years to identify patterns and improvement areas.

Explores how providers use machine learning and other technologies to detect and combat sales of these substances on their platforms.

Evaluates enforcement mechanisms by providers, such as user identification and account suspension.

Requires assessment of referrals to the Federal Government from providers, including investigations and outcomes.

Obligates submission of a report to Congress within one year detailing study findings and determinations.

Official name: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2023, Sec. 206 ("Study on Wildfire Detection Equipment and Integration of Artificial Intelligence Technologies")
Casual name: Western Wildfire Support Act, Sec. 206 ("Wildfire Detection Equipment and Integration of AI")
Short summary: Requires the Secretaries to study AI integration with wildfire detection technologies. Evaluates AI's impact on detection, management, and prediction of wildfires. Demands results be submitted to Congress and made publicly available within two years.
Long summary:
Mandates a study by the Secretaries on the effectiveness and limitations of wildfire detection technologies in various aspects of wildfire management.

Requires examination of how each technology, if deployed timely and properly, can effectively and efficiently address wildland fire threats.

Examines the integration of artificial intelligence with real-time imagery and weather data in wildfire detection technologies.

Explores how AI integration can enhance the value of these technologies, both individually and collectively.

Requires submission of the study results to congressional committees and public release within two years of the Act's enactment.

Official name: Americas Act, Title I ("Administration"), Sec. 103 ("Additional Duties of Institute")
Casual name: Americas Act, Title I ("Administration"), Sec. 103 ("Duties of Institute")
Short summary: Requires the Institute to promote international collaboration on e-governance, incorporate emerging technologies like AI, ensure compliance with standards, and restrict data sharing with specific countries to protect privacy and security. Authorizes funding and technical assistance for partner countries.
Long summary:
Mandates the Institute to promote international collaboration on e-governance systems with Americas partner countries.

Requires assistance in developing e-governance systems under the framework from section 102, including creating technical standards and reference implementations.

Facilitates shared infrastructure and technical assistance, including service development and contracts with partner countries.

Allows procurement or licensing of commercial technology for shared use and certifies audit organizations.

Permits partnerships with private entities and provides financial support for system development.

Accounts for emerging technologies, such as AI, advising on their integration or regulation.

Restricts procurement and data sharing under the e-governance framework with specific countries, including Russia and China, to ensure privacy and security.

Grants authority to exclude individuals, entities, or products not from approved countries to protect citizens’ privacy and security.

Official name: Investing in Tomorrow's Workforce Act of 2023
Casual name: Investing in Tomorrow's Workforce Act of 2023

Official name: A Bill to prohibit certain uses of automated decision systems by employers, and for other purposes.
Casual name: No Robot Bosses Act
Short summary: Restricts the use of automated decision systems for employment-related decisions, including by requiring such systems to be tested for efficacy, non-discrimination, and relevant laws & standards.
Long summary:
Prohibits employers from making any employment-related decisions -- including to fire or discipline an employee -- based solely on the output of an automated decision system.

Requires any automated decision system used to inform employment-related decisions to be tested before deployment for efficacy and compliance with relevant employment laws & the Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework from the National Institute of Standards & Technology. Systems must also be tested both before deployment and annually after deployment for non-discrimination on the basis of protected classes.

Requires employers to disclose to employees the use of automated decision systems for employment-related decisions and information on the workings of the system and the data used to reach its decision.

Establishes new roles and bodies in the Department of Labor to monitor and enforce this policy and to guide the development of future regulations on the use of automated decision systems in employment-related decision-making.

Establishes protections for whistleblowers who call attention to violations of this policy.

Official name: Sec. 2412. Duty of Care, Digital Consumer Protection Commission Act of 2023
Casual name: Sec. 2412 of 2023 Consumer Protection Act
Short summary: Prohibits non-governmental entities from designing or employing AI, or handling data, in ways that will harm people or discriminate either (a) on the basis of protected class or (b) in granting access to important opportunities and services.
Long summary:
Prohibits non-governmental entities from designing or employing AI, or handling personal data, in a way that will:

(1) Cause physical, psychological, economic, relational, or repetitional injuries to a person;

(2) Discriminate on the basis of standard protected classes, including race and nationality; or

(3) Discriminate in the granting of access to important services and opportunities, including employment, credit, and education.

Exempts private groups not open to the public from the requirements outlined above.

Official name: No CCP Consultants Act
Casual name: No CCP Consultants Act
Short summary: Prohibits the Department of State from working with contractors who have previously consulted for or fail to disclose conflicts of interest relating to foreign entities that pose national security or foreign policy concerns, including any Chinese state-owned entity or entity under the ownership and control of the PRC or CCP that is engaged in national security industries, such as producing AI-related products and services.
Long summary:
Prohibits the Department of State from entering, renewing, or extending a contract to contractors that, in the past year, have consulted for foreign entities that pose national security or foreign policy concerns, including any entity under China’s control engaged in national security industries, such as producing AI-related products and services. 

Requires companies seeking to provide consulting services for the Department of State to disclose any conflict of interest with foreign entities above, or they may face a debarment of up to five years.

Requires the Secretary of the Department of State to issue procurement policies for the Department to implement the above prohibition, clarify disclosure requirements and penalties when soliciting contracts, and share best practices for how companies can avoid accidentally contracting with foreign entities that pose national security or foreign policy concerns.

Official name: Drone Safety and Efficiency Act
Casual name: Drone Safety and Efficiency Act
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to adopt a performance and risk-based approach when reviewing requests for small unmanned aircraft systems (sUAS) to be waived from existing regulations.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration to adopt a performance and risk-based approach when reviewing requests for certificates of waiver under Section 107 of Title 14, Code of Federal Regulations, which establishes rules for the operation of small unmanned aircraft systems (sUAS) for commercial purposes. 

Requires the Administrator to use big data analytics and machine learning to conduct the performance and risk-based approach and refrain from using open-ended descriptive prompts in the waiver application.
Tags: Harms: Harm to health/safety;Harms: Harm to property

Official name: Artificial Intelligence and Biosecurity Risk Assessment Act
Casual name: AI Biorisk Assessment Act
Short summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response to conduct risk assessments and implement strategic initiatives that address threats to public health posed by AI, especially using AI to develop novel pathogens and bioweapons.
Long summary:
Requires the Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response to conduct risk assessments and implement strategic initiatives that address whether AI can develop novel pathogens, viruses, bioweapons, or chemical weapons, including monitoring and researching the global biological catastrophic risk landscape.

Requires the Assistant Secretary for Preparedness and Response to include a summary of the risk assessment above in the National Health Security Strategy.
Tags: Harms: Harm to health/safety;Harms: Financial loss;Harms: Harm to infrastructure;Strategies: Evaluation;Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Governance development;Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health

Official name: Sec. 2501. Corporate Citizenship and Ownership, Digital Consumer Protection Commission Act of 2023
Casual name: Sec. 2501 of 2023 Consumer Protection Act
Short summary: Requires operators of dominant online platforms with owners (including minority owners) who are citizens of foreign adversaries of the U.S. to sequester data and algorithms from agents of any foreign entity.
Long summary:
Requires operators of dominant online platforms with (a) large numbers of users and (b) ownership groups in which at least 10% of the members are citizens of foreign adversaries of the U.S. to sequester any back-end data or algorithm from agents of any foreign entity.

Also regulates foreign ownership of, and the granting of licenses for foreigners to operate, online platforms featuring large numbers of users, regardless of whether the platform uses AI.
Tags: Strategies: Input controls;Strategies: Input controls: Data circulation;Applications: Broadcasting and media production

Official name: Land Grant Research Prioritization Act of 2023
Casual name: Land Grant Research Prioritization Act of 2023
Short summary: Amends the Food, Agriculture, Conservation, and Trade Act of 1990 to allow research and extension grants to be made for developing agricultural applications of AI.
Long summary: Amends the Food, Agriculture, Conservation, and Trade Act of 1990 to allow research and extension grants to be made for developing and evaluating agricultural applications of AI, with an emphasis on the use of AI to improve specialty crop production.
Tags: Strategies: Government support;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Applications: Agriculture and resource extraction

Official name: Sensible Classification Act of 2023, Sec. 7: Implementation of Technology for Classification and Declassification
Casual name: Sensible Classification Act of 2023, Sec. 7: Implementation of Technology for Classification and Declassification
Short summary: Calls for using machine learning and artificial intelligence for both classification and declassification of national security information and security clearance eligibility assessment.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator of the Office of Electronic Government of the Office of Management and Budget, in consultation with various federal actors, to research a technology-based solution to reform the classification system employed by the federal government.

Requires a submission of recommendations to the President.

Mandates the President to submit a report on technology-based solutions to Congress no later than 540 days after the date of the enactment of this Act.
Tags: Strategies: Government study or report;Strategies: Government support: For R&D;Strategies: Convening;Strategies: New institution;Applications: Security;Applications: Government: military and public safety

Official name: Shifting Forward Vehicle Technologies Research and Development Act
Casual name: Vehicle Technologies R&D Act
Short summary: Establishes programs for AI-driven vehicle technology research, focusing on electrification, machine learning for manufacturing, autonomous systems, cybersecurity, and advanced energy solutions. Requires periodic reporting and coordination among federal agencies. Allocates funding for these initiatives through 2028.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Energy to conduct a research, development, and demonstration program for advanced vehicle technologies, focusing on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and vehicle manufacturing costs.

Mandates coordination with other federal agencies and existing programs on electric vehicle integration and critical material recycling.

Supports the development of advanced batteries, power electronics, electric drive systems, and vehicle electrification technologies.

Includes provisions for cybersecurity and physical security of vehicle interconnections with the electric grid and other infrastructure.

Establishes a program for medium- and heavy-duty commercial and transit vehicles, emphasizing advanced energy technologies and efficiency improvements.

Authorizes the creation of Graduate Automotive Technology Education Centers of Research Excellence to foster advancements in automotive energy technologies.

Allocates substantial funding for these initiatives through fiscal year 2028, with a total authorization exceeding $2.9 billion.

Official name: A bill to promote United States leadership in technical standards by directing the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the Department of State to take certain actions to encourage and enable United States participation in developing standards and specifications for artificial intelligence and other critical and emerging technologies, and for other purposes.
Casual name: S. 3849 (US Leadership in AI Standards)
Short summary: Requires NIST Director to brief Congress on federal AI standards participation within one year. Directs federal agencies to notify NIST of AI standards activities. Mandates NIST to create a reporting mechanism and establish a web portal. Establishes a pilot program for U.S.-based standards meetings. Obligates annual NIST briefings to Congress on the pilot program. Authorizes $10 million for the pilot program from 2024 to 2028.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to brief Congress within one year on federal participation opportunities in AI and emerging technology standards development.

Directs federal agencies to notify NIST of their involvement in AI standards activities and mandates NIST to create a reporting mechanism for this purpose.

Mandates NIST to establish a web portal to inform U.S. industry and federal agencies about international standards efforts and participation opportunities.

Establishes a pilot program for supporting U.S.-based standards meetings, including prestandardization and coordination meetings, through grants to eligible entities.

Obligates NIST to brief Congress annually on the pilot program’s effectiveness and provide recommendations for permanent implementation if deemed feasible.

Authorizes $10 million for the pilot program from fiscal years 2024 through 2028.

Official name: Exploitative Workplace Surveillance and Technologies Task Force Act of 2023
Casual name: Exploitative Workplace Technologies Task Force Act
Short summary: Establishes an interagency task force to study workplace surveillance and AI decision systems. Evaluates surveillance's impact on various worker aspects, including compensation and safety. Requires reports and recommendations on addressing surveillance and AI technologies in workplaces.
Long summary:
Establishes the White House Task Force on Workplace Surveillance and Technologies within 180 days of enactment.

Mandates the Task Force to study and evaluate the use of workplace surveillance and automated decision systems by employers.

Requires the Task Force to analyze the prevalence, types, and impacts of workplace surveillance across industries, including data collection, storage, security, and transfer practices.

Necessitates examination of the effects of workplace surveillance on worker compensation, performance reviews, scheduling, career advancement, safety, health coverage, and discipline.

Obligates the Task Force to consider the impact on vulnerable populations, including workers with disabilities, low-wage workers, and workers of color.

Orders the Task Force to submit initial, interim, and final reports to Congress, detailing findings and providing recommendations on addressing workplace surveillance and related technologies.

Terminates the Task Force 60 days after submitting the final report.

Official name: Jobs of the Future Act of 2023
Casual name: Jobs of the Future Act of 2023
Short summary:
Requires the Secretary of Labor and the NSF Director to submit interim and final reports on AI's workforce impact. 

Mandates identification of necessary data, affected industries, and vulnerable demographics. 

Requires analysis of skills and education needed to work with AI.

Demands recommendations to mitigate displacement and promote equitable AI education. 

Requires collaboration with educational institutions, industry stakeholders, and federal agencies.
Long summary:
Requires the Secretary of Labor and the Director of the National Science Foundation to submit interim and final reports on AI's impact on the U.S. workforce.

Mandates these reports to identify data necessary for analyzing AI's workforce impact, industries most affected by AI, and demographics vulnerable to job displacement.

Requires analysis of skills, expertise, and education needed to work alongside AI and methods to deliver these to the workforce.

Demands recommendations to mitigate workforce displacement, promote knowledge sharing, and ensure underserved demographics have access to necessary AI-related education.

Requires collaboration with educational institutions, industry stakeholders, and federal agencies in preparing the reports.

Official name: Digital Defense Content Provenance Act of 2023
Casual name: Digital Defense Content Provenance Act
Short summary: Requires the DMA Director to establish a DINFOS course on digital content provenance within one year. Mandates coverage of forgery challenges and authentication standards. Obligates a pilot program with an interim report by January 1, 2026, ending January 1, 2027. Defines key terms.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the Defense Media Activity (DMA) to establish a course of education at the Defense Information School (DINFOS) on digital content provenance within one year of enactment.

Mandates the course to cover digital content provenance, challenges posed by digital content forgery, and the use of industry open technical standards for authenticating digital content.

Obligates the Director to start a pilot program within one year to assess the implementation of industry standards for digital content provenance for official Department media.

Sets termination date for the pilot program on January 1, 2027, with an interim report due by January 1, 2026, to assess effectiveness and feasibility.

Defines key terms including "digital content forgery" and "digital content provenance" to ensure clarity in implementation.

Official name: AI for National Security Act
Casual name: AI for National Security Act
Short summary: Amends Section 1521 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 to expand the Department of Defense's procurement of cyber data products and services to include AI-driven endpoint security and to strengthen software supply chain security.
Long summary:
Amends Section 1521 of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2022 to expand the Department of Defense's procurement of cyber data products and services to include AI-driven endpoint security and to strengthen software supply chain security.

Inserts a provision to enhance the security of the software supply chain of the Department of Defense using AI.
Tags: Applications: Government: military and public safety;Risk factors: Security: Cybersecurity;Strategies: Government support

Official name: Justice in Forensic Algorithms Act of 2024
Casual name: Justice in Forensic Algorithms Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology to establish a program to create and maintain testing standards for computational forensic software, including AI-enabled software for evidence analysis.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to establish a program (“Testing Standards”) to create and maintain testing standards for computational forensic software, which includes software that relies on AI-enabled automated or semiautomated computational processes to analyze or interpret evidence.

Requires Testing Standards to solicit public input, address the potential for disparate impacts on protected groups, and specify requirements for publicly available documentation by developers, validation processes by those using computational forensic software, reports provided to defendants regarding software used, and how to test software and report results. 

Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to establish a program (“Testing Program”) that tests computational forensic algorithms according to the testing standards, using realistic and representative sample data and adversarial examples, and publishing results. Requires software to be re-tested after significant changes. 

Requires Federal law enforcement agencies only to use computational forensic software tested by NIST’s Testing Program and only after conducting an internal validation and making results publicly available. 

Requires evidence from computational forensic software to be admissible only if it has been submitted to NIST’s Testing Program and the developers waive legal claims against the defense analyzing or testing it. Requires information about the software (e.g., source code) and results from analyses by the software to be shared with the defendant.

Official name: ASK Act
Casual name: ASK Act
Short summary: Directs the FCC, in consultation with the FTC, to issue rules prohibiting the offer of artificial intelligence products to minors without parental consent.
Long summary:
Directs the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), in consultation with the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), to issue rules prohibiting the offer of artificial intelligence products to minors without parental consent.

Mandates the rule to include a provision allowing parents and guardians to revoke consent at any time.

Official name: Autonomous Vehicle Accessibility Act/AV Accessibility Act
Casual name: Autonomous Vehicle Accessibility Act/AV Accessibility Act
Short summary: Prohibits states from issuing licenses for Level 4 or 5 ADS-equipped vehicle operation that discriminate against individuals with disabilities. Requires the Secretary to study infrastructure improvements for accessible ride-hail ADS-equipped vehicles. Authorizes $5 million for this study.
Long summary:
Cites the Act as the “Autonomous Vehicle Accessibility Act” or the “AV Accessibility Act”.

Defines key terms including “disability,” “public transportation,” and “ride-hail ADS-equipped vehicle.”

Prohibits states from issuing licenses for Level 4 or Level 5 ADS-equipped vehicles in a manner that discriminates against individuals with disabilities, in accordance with the ADA.

Mandates the Secretary of Transportation to commission a study through the National Academies to identify changes needed in public transportation infrastructure to improve accessibility for individuals with disabilities using ride-hail ADS-equipped vehicles.

Authorizes $5,000,000 for the completion of the accessibility infrastructure study, available until expended.

Official name: Healthy Technology Act of 2023
Casual name: Healthy Technology Act of 2023
Short summary: Authorizes artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies to prescribe pharmaceutical drugs if approved by the State involved and the Food and Drug Administration
Long summary:
Amends the definition of 'practitioner licensed by law to administer such a drug' in Section 503(b) of the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act, to include artificial intelligence and machine learning technology

Authorizes AI and ML technology to prescribe pharmaceutical drugs if approved by the State in question, and if approved by the Food and Drug Administration 

Requires such AI and ML technology to be approved by the Food and Drug Administration through conventional channels, including having to provide 90 days' premarket notification if this is deemed a new device
Tags: Applications: Medicine, life sciences and public health;Strategies: Evaluation

Official name: To establish a Joint Autonomy Office in the Department of Defense, and for other purposes.
Casual name: HR3168, 2023, Joint Autonomy Office Establishment
Short summary: Establishes the Joint Autonomy Office in the Department of Defense, responsible for coordinating and accelerating the adoption of all-domain autonomous systems for operational users, and for developing a Department-wide classification framework for autonomous capabilities.
Long summary:
Directs the Secretary of Defense to establish the Joint Autonomy Office in the Department of Defense, responsible for coordinating and accelerating the adoption of all-domain autonomous systems for operational users, including through the adoption of commercial and defense best practices

Requires the Office to implement an all-domain enterprise autonomous systems software development and testing platform across DoD's classification levels, including an all-domain data acquisition and curation process to build and manage datasets; these datasets and platforms should be released to various services and acquisition programs as they mature.

Requires the Secretary of Defense to ensure that the Office has unlimited access to existing datasets regardless of classification level, and to introduce security protocols to protect data shared with or maintained by the Office.

Requires the Director of the Office must establish a Department-wide classification framework for autonomous capabilities, considering the complexity of autonomous systems, the necessity of human supervision or control, their applicability to various warfighting domains, and costs; the Director must submit this framework along with an explanation of its criteria and requirements to Congress, with updates released no less frequently than once every two years.

Requires the Director to develop and implement plans to standardize planning, resource and integration of autonomous capabilities across the Department; this includes a report assessing the Department's current efforts which identifies acquisition, training and evaluation gaps, as well as recommendations to address these.

Official name: Disincentivizing Internet Service Censorship of Online Users and Restrictions on Speech and Expression Act
Casual name: DISCOURSE Act
Short summary: Amends Section 230 to limit liability protections for dominant service providers. Deems providers as content creators if they manage content or use targeted algorithms with a discernible viewpoint. Excludes protections for actions against religious material. Mandates public disclosure of content management practices. Reclassifies immunity as an affirmative defense.
Long summary:
Amends Section 230 of the Communications Act of 1934 to limit liability protections for dominant interactive computer service providers based on their content management practices.

Deems providers as content creators if they engage in content moderation or use targeted algorithmic amplification that expresses, promotes, or suppresses a discernible viewpoint.

Excludes liability protections for actions against religious material that burden the exercise of religion.

Mandates public disclosure of content management mechanisms and practices by providers to enable informed consumer choices and entrepreneurial activities.

Reclassifies immunity under Section 230 as an affirmative defense, requiring providers or users to prove they are not information content providers in legal actions.
Tags: Incentives: Civil liability

Official name: Advisory for AI-Generated Content Act
Casual name: Advisory for AI-Generated Content Act
Short summary: Requires all entities that generate AI materials for public consumption to label them with watermarks.
Long summary:
Requires all AI-generated materials to be labeled with a watermark. 

Authorizes the federal government to establish requirements and standards for watermarks.

Enables the federal government to levy penalties against entities that violate the above stipulation.

Defines key terms including AI-generating entity, AI-generated material, and covered AI-generated material, specifying criteria for materials that must be watermarked.

Official name: AI LEAD Act
Casual name: AI LEAD Act
Short summary: Establishes the CAIO Council to coordinate federal AI practices and risk management. Mandates each agency appoint a CAIO and requires AI Governance Boards in agencies.
Long summary:
Establishes the Chief Artificial Intelligence Officers (CAIO) Council to coordinate federal AI practices, interagency activities, and risk management. Mandates each federal agency to appoint a Chief Artificial Intelligence Officer responsible for promoting AI innovation, policy development, and risk management.

Requires the creation of Artificial Intelligence Governance Boards within each agency to oversee AI issues and ensure compliance with relevant laws and standards. Obligates agencies to develop AI strategies emphasizing trustworthy adoption, risk mitigation, transparency, and the protection of individual rights. Directs the Comptroller General to report on the effectiveness of the AI Governance Boards and CAIOs within two years of enactment.

Official name: AI Foundation Model Transparency Act of 2023
Casual name: AI Foundation Model Transparency Act of 2023
Short summary: Directs the Federal Trade Commission to issue transparency regulations for AI model training data and algorithms in consultation with relevant stakeholders within 9 months. Specifies the information categories to be publicly disclosed and updated annually.
Long summary:
Directs the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to establish and routinely update standards for making training data and algorithms of AI foundation models publicly available.

Requires the FTC to promulgate regulations within 9 months and specifies information to be disclosed, including sources of training data and model performance evaluations.

Mandates consultation with relevant stakeholders, including NIST, OSTP, and the Register of Copyrights, in establishing these standards.

Treats violations of these regulations as violations under the FTC Act regarding unfair or deceptive acts or practices.

Authorizes $10 million for fiscal year 2025 and $3 million annually thereafter for implementation.

Official name: Artificial Intelligence Environmental Impacts Act of 2024
Casual name: Artificial Intelligence Environmental Impacts Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency to submit a report to Congress on the environmental impacts of artificial intelligence
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to study  the environmental impacts of artificial intelligence, with a report on its findings submitted to Congress within two years of the Act's enactment.

Mandates that the EPA study examine the energy, water consumption, and pollution throughout the full lifecycle of AI models, hardware, and data centers, considering the effects of location, power sources, and AI model design on these environmental impacts.

Requires that the EPA study assess the positive environmental impacts associated with the use of AI, such as optimizing systems for energy efficiency or automatically monitoring environmental changes.

Directs the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to work with other government officials to form a consortium comprising academic, civil society, and industry representatives to assess and report on AI's environmental impacts, including the feasibility of open-source tools for measurement and recommendations to mitigate negative environmental effects.

Instructs the Director of NIST to work with other government officials and the consortium to establish a voluntary reporting system for entities to report on their AI systems' environmental impact.

Requires the Administrator of EPA, Secretary of Energy and Director of NIST to submit a joint report on the consortium's findings and its proposed voluntary reporting system within four years of the Act's enactment.

Official name: A bill to waive immunity under section 230 of the Communications Act of 1934 for claims and charges related to generative artificial intelligence.
Casual name: General AI Communications Act Immunity
Short summary: Removes Section 230 protection (federal liability protection) for providers of interactive computer services if the conduct giving rise to liability involves generative artificial intelligence.
Long summary: Amends Section 230 of the Communications Act of 1934 (which prevents providers of an interactive computer service from being held legally liable for third-party content) to remove this protection for claims involving the use or provision of generative artificial intelligence.

Official name: United States-Israel Health Technologies Cooperation Act
Casual name: US-Israel Health Technologies Act
Short summary: Authorizes $4,000,000 annually (2024-2026) for a bilateral health tech program with Israel emphasizing AI, drugs, vaccinations, and telemedicine. Requires matching Israeli contributions.
Long summary:
Authorizes $4,000,000 annually for fiscal years 2024 through 2026 to fund a bilateral cooperative program with Israel for developing health technologies. Emphasizes advancing technology, personalized medicine, and data usage in relation to emerging infectious diseases.

Includes artificial intelligence, drugs, vaccinations, respiratory assist devices, diagnostic tests, and telemedicine as eligible health technologies. Requires matching contributions from the Government of Israel to access funding. Allows funding for existing bilateral programs or the establishment of new programs in consultation with the Secretary of State under the 2008 Science and Technology Cooperation Agreement.

Official name: Protecting the Information of our Vulnerable Adolescents, Children, and Youth Act
Casual name: Kids PRIVACY Act
Short summary: Defines "process" as an operation, potentially automated, performed on "covered information" including collecting, disclosing, inferring, etc. Mandates "verifiable consent" before processing of data, data minimization, data access transparency, security measures, and limits to harmful practices.
Long summary:
Defines "process" as an operation, potentially automated, performed on "covered information" including collecting, disclosing, inferring, etc.

Mandates obtaining "verifiable consent" from teenagers or parents before processing their data.

Requires operators to minimize data collection and retain data only as necessary.

Obligates transparency and the provision of a clear privacy policy detailing data practices.

Prohibits targeted advertising and harmful data processing practices.

Establishes rights for teenagers and parents to access, correct, and delete data.

Imposes strict security measures for processing children's and teenagers' data.

Official name: FinCEN Modernization Act of 2023
Casual name: FinCEN Modernization Act of 2023
Short summary: Cites the Act as the "FinCEN Modernization Act of 2023." Requires FinCEN to establish research and information-sharing programs on technological advances. Mandates exploration of beneficial innovations. Obligates adoption of necessary technologies. Enables information exchange. Authorizes non-standard transactions. Compels annual reporting to Congress.
Long summary:
Cites the Act as the "FinCEN Modernization Act of 2023."

Requires FinCEN to establish research, development, and information-sharing programs focusing on technological advances like machine learning and data analytics.

Mandates FinCEN to explore and evaluate technological innovations beneficial for financial systems and detect illicit activities.

Obligates FinCEN to adopt necessary technologies for monitoring cryptocurrencies and addressing money laundering and cyber threats.

Enables FinCEN to exchange information with other Treasury offices and federal departments.

Authorizes FinCEN to enter into non-standard transactions and accept contributions, gifts, or donations to support technological advancements.

Compels FinCEN to submit an annual report detailing transactions, contributions, and their justifications and purposes to relevant Congressional committees.
Tags: Strategies: Government support: For R&D

Official name: Eliminating Bias in Algorithmic Systems Act of 2023
Casual name: Eliminating Bias in Algorithmic Systems Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires agencies that use, fund, or oversee algorithms to establish civil rights offices with bias experts. Mandates biennial reports to Congress on algorithmic bias, risks and actions taken to mitigate them.
Long summary:
Requires agencies which use or oversee a covered algorithm to have an office of civil rights employing experts and technologists on bias, discrimination, and algorithmic harms.

Mandates these offices to submit biennial reports to Congress detailing risks from use of algorithms using artificial intelligence techniques within the agency's jurisdiction, mitigation steps, actions taken to engage stakeholders, and recommendations for further mitigation of algorithmic bias and discrimination.

Establishes an interagency working group on covered algorithms and civil rights, led by the Department of Justice's Civil Rights Division.

Official name: AI Disclosure Act of 2023
Casual name: AI Disclosure Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires generative AI to include a disclaimer on outputs and subjects violators to penalties and grants privileges.
Long summary:
Requires generative artificial intelligence to include a disclaimer on any output. Mandates the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to enforce this disclosure requirement.

Classifies violations of the disclosure requirement as unfair or deceptive acts or practices under the Federal Trade Commission Act.Empowers the FTC to enforce the disclosure requirement using the same means, jurisdiction, powers, and duties as provided in the Federal Trade Commission Act. Subjects violators to penalties and grants them privileges and immunities in accordance with the Federal Trade Commission Act.

Official name: Federal Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Act of 2023
Casual name: Federal Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to issue guidance on how federal agencies can use NIST’s AI Risk Management Framework to reduce risks.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) and Director of the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) to issue guidance on how federal agencies can use NIST’s AI Risk Management Framework (RMF) to reduce risks, covering cybersecurity for AI systems, requirements for AI suppliers, and training for agencies.

Requires the Comptroller General of the United States to assess the impact of NIST's AI RMF on agencies' AI use within a year, and requires the Director of OMB to report to Congress on agency implementation and adherence to NIST’s AI RMF within a year, and subsequently, at least once every three years.

Excludes national security systems from the requirements above to adhere to NIST’s AI RMF.

Requires the Federal Acquisition Regulatory Council to issue guidance on agency AI procurement within a year, to ensure compliance with NIST’s AI RMF.

Requires the Director of OMB to establish an initiative to build agencies’ AI expertise, focusing on hiring interdisciplinary experts and incorporating best practices from existing initiatives.

Requires the Director of NIST to conduct a study on and convene stakeholders to develop voluntary consensus standards to test and evaluate AI acquisitions, develop methods and conduct evaluations, monitor evaluations and adjust methods, and make recommendations to each agency on risks from relevant AI acquisitions.

Official name: CONSULT Act of 2023
Casual name: CONSULT Act
Short summary: Prohibits the Department of Defense from awarding contracts to any individual or entity, including those producing AI-powered products and services, that has entered into working agreements with any organization from a blacklist of national security threats.
Long summary:
Requires the Department of Defense to refrain from awarding contracts to any individual or entity, including those producing AI-powered products and services, that has worked with any organization from a blacklist of national security threats.

Authorizes the DOD to punish any contractor of the Department who has a proven conflict of interest with the DOD and fails to disclose that conflict of interest.

Official name: Political BIAS Emails Act of 2023
Casual name: Political BIAS Emails Act of 2023
Short summary: Prohibits email providers from flagging elected political emails as spam without user direction. Requires quarterly transparency reports on flagged political emails. Mandates data disclosure to campaigns upon request. Obliges providers to offer email deliverability best practices. Enforces via FTC. Defines key terms.
Long summary:
Prohibits email service providers from using filtering algorithms to flag emails from political campaigns that consumers have elected to receive as spam without user direction.

Requires providers to publish quarterly transparency reports detailing instances of political emails flagged as spam, including breakdowns by political party and filtering algorithm usage.

Mandates email providers to disclose specific spam-filtering data to political campaigns upon request, with limitations on request frequency.

Obliges providers to offer best practices to political campaigns for improving email deliverability to primary inboxes.

Enforces these provisions through the Federal Trade Commission, treating violations as unfair or deceptive acts under the Federal Trade Commission Act.

Defines key terms including “filtering algorithm,” “operator,” and “political campaign.”

Official name: Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act of 2023
Casual name: Promoting Resilient Supply Chains Act
Short summary: Requires the Assistant Secretary of Commerce to promote U.S. leadership in AI and strengthen supply chains. Mandates a resiliency program. Requires coordination with agencies. Promotes domestic manufacturing. Supports best practices. Mandates regular reporting on AI supply chains.
Long summary:
Requires the Assistant Secretary of Commerce to promote U.S. leadership in emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI), and strengthen critical supply chains and national security.

Mandates the establishment of a critical supply chain resiliency and crisis response program within the Department of Commerce, which includes mapping and monitoring AI supply chains.

Requires coordination with other agencies, industry, and international partners to identify vulnerabilities and prepare for supply chain shocks affecting AI technologies.

Promotes the growth of domestic manufacturing capacities for AI and other emerging technologies and encourages the relocation of manufacturing facilities to the U.S. or allied countries.

Supports the development and dissemination of best practices for supply chain resilience, including the use of blockchain and other technologies to authenticate and trace critical goods.

Mandates regular reporting on the state of AI and other emerging technology supply chains, including assessments of vulnerabilities and recommendations for enhancing resilience.

Official name: DATA Privacy Act
Casual name: DATA Privacy Act
Short summary: Establishes national data privacy standards for covered entities. Requires clear privacy notices. Mandates FTC regulations within one year. Imposes data minimization requirements. Empowers individuals with data rights. Mandates privacy officers for large entities. Grants FTC and state enforcement authority. Allocates FTC resources.
Long summary:
Establishes national data privacy standards for covered entities handling personal data.

Requires covered entities to post clear, accessible privacy notices detailing data collection, processing, storage, and disclosure practices.

Mandates the FTC to create regulations within one year for reasonable, equitable, and non-deceptive data practices, including opt-out and affirmative consent mechanisms.

Imposes data minimization requirements, limiting data collection to what is necessary for specified purposes and ensuring timely deletion.

Empowers individuals with rights to access, correct, delete, and transfer their data.

Mandates large entities to appoint privacy protection officers to oversee compliance and advocate for privacy policies.

Directs the National Science Foundation to support research on privacy-enhancing technologies.

Grants enforcement authority to the FTC and state attorneys general, with civil penalties for violations.

Allocates resources to the FTC for enforcement and compliance activities.

Official name: Outbound Investment Transparency Act of 2023
Casual name: Outbound Investment Transparency Act
Short summary: Amends the Defense Production Act to establish an investment screening mechanism targeting AI and other sensitive sectors. Requires U.S. persons to notify the Treasury of certain investments in countries of concern. Mandates annual reporting and promotes international coordination.
Long summary:
Requires mandatory notification by U.S. persons engaging in specific foreign investments related to advanced sectors, including artificial intelligence, to the Secretary of the Treasury.

Defines covered activities involving equity interests, debt obligations with governance rights, and joint ventures in countries of concern, with specific focus on AI and other advanced technologies.

Establishes penalties for non-compliance, including failure to notify or providing false information.

Mandates the creation of regulations to ensure clarity and minimize compliance burdens, with a focus on transparency and stakeholder involvement.

Directs the Secretary of State to engage with allied countries to coordinate investment screening mechanisms and share information on covered activities.

Requires annual reporting to Congress on trends, national security risks, and recommendations related to these foreign investments in AI and other sectors.

Official name: TEST AI Act of 2023
Casual name: TEST AI Act of 2023
Short summary: Requires the NIST Director to establish testbeds to support the development of trustworthy AI systems, examine vulnerabilities in existing systems, and build guardrails for harm prevention.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the National Institute on Standards and Technology (NIST) to work with other heads of federal agencies and private/public sector stakeholders to build testbeds for supporting the development of trustworthy AI systems and AI guardrails.

Mandates the Secretary of Commerce and Secretary of Energy to enter into a memorandum of understanding that allows relevant agencies across the federal government access to AI-related personnel, facilities, and research at the Department of Energy.
