Official name: Atomic Supply Chain Solutions Act
Casual name: Atomic Supply Chain Solutions Act
Short summary: Analyzes potential AI applications in the nuclear supply chain, including cybersecurity, efficiency, maintenance, and workforce training. Evaluates AI's role in identifying counterfeit components and economic considerations. Considers regulatory challenges related to AI usage in the nuclear industry.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Energy to evaluate the U.S. nuclear supply chain within 270 days, including past efforts, future needs, and lessons from projects like Vogtle 3 and 4.

Analyzes regulatory challenges, including the necessity of N-stamps for nuclear components, cost implications, and potential certification streamlining.

Considers advanced manufacturing and innovative technologies like AI, 3D printing, and robotics for enhancing the nuclear supply chain, with attention to cybersecurity, efficiency, and workforce training.

Describes U.S.-Canada cooperation on nuclear components, assessing competitiveness and recommending collaboration to enhance North American nuclear leadership.

Assesses the impact of anticipated demand for advanced nuclear technology on the supply chain, proposing strategies for resilience.

Establishes U.S. policy to prioritize a robust HALEU market to strengthen energy independence and national security, addressing risks associated with increased nuclear technology demand.

Official name: Veterans Online Information and Cybersecurity Empowerment Act of 2024
Casual name: VOICE Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes a grant program to enhance veterans' digital literacy and cybersecurity skills. Promotes media literacy, critical thinking, and understanding of foreign influence tactics. Requires grant recipients and the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to report on program effectiveness. Allocates $20 million for specified fiscal years.
Long summary:
Establishes a grant program to promote digital citizenship and media literacy among veterans, requiring the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to award grants to eligible entities.

Mandates grant recipients to develop cybersecurity best practices and improve veterans' competencies in digital and media literacy.

Requires grant recipients to submit a report on their activities and effectiveness one year after receiving funds.

Obliges the Secretary to report to Congress on the program's overall activities and effectiveness after receiving the last report from grant recipients.

Authorizes $20 million in appropriations for fiscal years 2023, 2025, and 2027 to fund the program.

Defines key terms such as "cybersecurity best practices," "digital citizenship," "media literacy," and "eligible entity."

Encourages the Secretary to maintain a list of grant recipients and promote communication among them.

Acknowledges the role of foreign influence campaigns and online scams targeting veterans as critical issues that need addressing.

Official name: Agriculture and National Security Act of 2024
Casual name: Agriculture and National Security Act of 2024
Short summary: Amends the Department of Agriculture Reorganization Act to require biennial reports assessing national security gaps, including AI and cybersecurity. Establishes a Senior Advisor for National Security to coordinate activities and improve interagency collaboration on food and agriculture security vulnerabilities.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Agriculture to prioritize national security alongside homeland security within the Department of Agriculture, including increasing staff with security clearances.

Establishes the position of Senior Advisor for National Security to advise the Secretary and coordinate with the National Security Council on national security matters related to food and agriculture.

Authorizes the Secretary to exchange personnel with defense, national security, law enforcement, and intelligence agencies to improve information sharing and risk mitigation.

Requires biennial reports to Congress and the National Security Council assessing national security gaps in the food and agriculture sector, including cybersecurity and AI, and detailing actions taken and recommendations for policy changes.

Addresses vulnerabilities concerning agricultural data, foreign influence, and emerging technologies.

Official name: A bill to require the Secretary of Defense to carry out a pilot program on using artificial intelligence-enabled software to optimize the workflow and operations of depots, shipyards, and other manufacturing facilities run by the Department of Defense, and for other purposes.
Casual name: A bill to require the Secretary of Defense to carry out a pilot program on using artificial intelligence-enabled software to optimize the workflow and operations of depots, shipyards, and other manufacturing facilities run by the Department of Defense, and for other purposes.
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Defense to initiate a pilot program using AI-enabled software for optimizing Department of Defense facility operations. Mandates a $35 million minimum expenditure and consultation with military branches. Obligates a detailed evaluation report within one year.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Defense to initiate a pilot program within 60 days to evaluate artificial intelligence-enabled software for optimizing workflow and operations in Department of Defense facilities, including depots and shipyards.

Requires the assessment of AI software for contract management, particularly by the Defense Contract Management Agency.

Directs the use of best-in-class software platforms and consideration of industry best practices, alongside human-centered design methodologies.

Obligates a minimum expenditure of $35,000,000 for the pilot program.

Requires consultation with the Under Secretary of Defense for Acquisition and Sustainment and the Secretaries of the Army, Navy, and Air Force.

Mandates a report to Congress within one year detailing software evaluations, workflow modifications, and quantitative impacts observed during the pilot.

Official name: Workforce of the Future Act of 2024
Casual name: Workforce of the Future Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Education to award grants for expanding AI and technology education from pre-K to high school. Directs the Secretary of Labor and others to report on AI's workforce impact, emphasizing skills development. Authorizes funding for workforce upskilling and education.
Long summary:
Mandates the preparation of reports by the Secretary of Labor, the National Science Foundation, and the Secretary of Education on AI's workforce impact, identifying data needs, affected industries, and opportunities for stakeholder influence.

Authorizes the Department of Education to award grants to enhance emerging and advanced technology education from prekindergarten through grade 12, focusing on inclusivity for underrepresented communities.

Requires the Department of Labor to grant funds for upskilling workers in industries significantly impacted by AI, promoting lifelong learning and skill certifications.

Prescribes continuous evaluation and monitoring of grant-funded activities, with a focus on sustaining project activities post-grant.

Demands biannual reporting by grantees on fund usage, disaggregated by demographic factors, and a comprehensive report to Congress within five years.

Amends existing education laws to integrate emerging technology education data, emphasizing competency in AI-related skills among students.

Official name: Modernizing Retrospective Regulatory Review Act
Casual name: Modernizing Retrospective Regulatory Review Act
Short summary: Requires the Office of Management and Budget to issue guidance on using AI and technology for retrospective regulatory reviews. Mandates agencies to submit and implement plans for reviewing regulations using this guidance, identifying obsolete or burdensome regulations.
Long summary:
Requires the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to report on the progress of making agency regulations available in machine-readable format within 180 days.

Mandates the issuance of guidance within 18 months on using technology, including AI, for conducting retrospective reviews of agency regulations.

Instructs agencies to use technology to identify obsolete, ineffective, or problematic regulations and adequately train personnel in its use.

Obliges agency heads to submit a detailed plan within two years for implementing the guidance, including identifying regulations for review.

Compels agency heads to implement the strategy for retrospective review within 180 days after plan submission.

Official name: SAFE Bet Act
Casual name: SAFE Bet Act
Short summary: Prohibits the use of AI in tracking wagers, creating targeted promotions, and developing gambling products in sports wagering operations. Requires sports wagering programs to include consumer protection, integrity standards, and restrictions on AI use. Establishes a national self-exclusion list and mandates annual surveys on problem gambling.
Long summary:
Establishes the "SAFE Bet Act," setting minimum federal standards for sports wagering, which includes restrictions on AI use by sports wagering operators. 

Prohibits operators from using AI to track individual wagers, target specific individuals with promotions, or create gambling products like microbets. 

Mandates state sports wagering programs to ensure compliance with standards, including consumer protections and advertising restrictions, and requires operators to maintain data security and recordkeeping. 

Enforces reporting of suspicious transactions and mandates cooperation with investigations into sports wagering activities. 

Creates a national self-exclusion list to prevent individuals from placing sports wagers across states, and establishes public health initiatives to monitor gambling addiction. 

Permits states and tribes to enact stricter sports wagering regulations and does not preempt their authority to tax sports wagering.

Official name: Department of Energy AI Act
Casual name: Department of Energy AI Act
Short summary: Establishes a centralized AI program within the Department of Energy to advance AI research and deployment for science, security, and technology. Requires development of AI models, datasets, and infrastructure. Mandates AI risk evaluation, workforce development, and partnerships. Allocates $2.4 billion annually for five years.
Long summary:
Establishes the "Department of Energy AI Act" to advance AI capabilities within the Department of Energy (DOE).

Requires the Secretary of Energy to create a centralized AI program focused on research and deployment of AI for science, security, and technology.

Mandates the development of AI training datasets, next-generation computing infrastructure, and safe AI models.

Directs the establishment of at least eight multidisciplinary AI Research and Development Centers to enhance U.S. leadership in AI.

Implements an AI Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Program to address safety and security concerns.

Allocates funds for STEM education and workforce development, emphasizing AI skill training and diversity.

Establishes a program to use AI to improve federal permitting processes for energy projects.

Initiates rulemaking to standardize AI use in grid interconnection.

Requires a report on the impact of AI on data centers and energy security.

Creates the Office of Critical and Emerging Technology to coordinate AI efforts across the DOE and assess technological impacts on national security.

Official name: Preventing the Algorithmic Facilitation of Rental Housing Cartels Act of 2024
Casual name: Preventing the Algorithmic Facilitation of Rental Housing Cartels Act of 2024
Short summary: Prohibits rental property owners from using coordinators to manipulate rental prices. Prohibits coordinators from facilitating non-competitive agreements and engaging in anti-competitive mergers. Empowers the FTC and state attorneys general to enforce the Act.
Long summary:
Prohibits rental property owners from using coordinators for algorithmic price coordination, deeming such actions per se violations of the Sherman Act.

Prohibits coordinators from facilitating non-competitive agreements among rental property owners or performing coordinating functions.

Prohibits coordinators from acquiring another coordinator if it lessens competition, violating the Clayton Act.

Empowers the Federal Trade Commission, the Attorney General, and State Attorneys General to enforce the Act with the same powers and duties as the FTC Act, Sherman Act, and Clayton Act.

Allows civil actions by injured persons to recover treble damages and litigation costs, with the option to invalidate pre-dispute arbitration agreements.

Clarifies that the Act does not preempt State, Tribal, or local laws that supplement its provisions.

Ensures severability of the Act if any provision is deemed unconstitutional, maintaining the effectiveness of remaining provisions.

Official name: Detection Equipment and Technology Evaluation to Counter the Threat of Fentanyl and Xylazine Act of 2024
Casual name: DETECT Fentanyl and Xylazine Act of 2024
Short summary: Amends the Homeland Security Act to enhance drug detection capabilities using AI, requiring research and development of equipment for law enforcement. Directs adherence to AI risk management guidelines and prioritization based on DEA threat reports.
Long summary:
Amends the Homeland Security Act of 2002 to enhance the detection and disruption of drugs like fentanyl and xylazine using advanced technologies.

Mandates research and development of equipment for drug detection and trafficking disruption, emphasizing minimal handling and separation of complex mixtures.

Incorporates machine learning and artificial intelligence to predict controlled substance analogues and new psychoactive substances.

Requires the Under Secretary for Science and Technology to adhere to the NIST AI Risk Management Framework or its successor.

Establishes research and evaluation priorities using the latest information from reports on drug threats by the Drug Enforcement Administration.

Ensures federal, state, local, tribal, and territorial law enforcement agencies have access to improved detection technologies.

Official name: Content Origin Protection and Integrity from Edited and Deepfaked Media Act of 2024
Casual name: Content Origin Protection and Integrity from Edited and Deepfaked Media Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires transparency in AI-generated content, mandates development of standards for content provenance, and prohibits tampering with provenance information. Enforces compliance through FTC and state actions. Penalizes unauthorized AI training using non-consensual content. Establishes public-private partnerships for developing standards.
Long summary:
Mandates the development of standards for content provenance and detection of synthetic and synthetically-modified content through a public-private partnership.

Requires tools used commercially to create synthetic or synthetically-modified content to provide the option of including machine-readable content provenance information, with security measures to prevent tampering.

Prohibits knowingly removing or altering content provenance information in furtherance of unfair or deceptive acts.

Prohibits the non-consensual use of content with attached provenance information for training AI systems, requiring express consent and adherence to copyright terms.

Empowers the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general to enforce the Act, treating violations as unfair or deceptive acts.

Permits private parties to bring civil actions for violations involving content provenance information.

Ensures the Act does not alter existing copyright rights and includes provisions for severability, maintaining the Act’s effectiveness even if parts are invalidated.

Official name: Forecasting Optimization for Robust Earth Climate Analysis and Subseasonal-to-Seasonal Tracking Act of 2024
Casual name: FORECASTS Tracking Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes the Weather and Earth System Modeling and Data Assimilation Workforce Innovation Program to advance AI in forecasting. Prioritizes multi-model ensemble systems, emerging technologies, and workforce development. Authorizes significant appropriations for research and operational advancements in weather prediction.
Long summary:
Amends the Food Security Act of 1985 to improve subseasonal-to-seasonal weather forecasting by requiring the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) to enhance research, data collection, and model development.

Mandates the development of multi-model ensemble forecast systems and data management strategies, and the operationalization of new modeling technologies.

Establishes an Internet clearinghouse for forecasts and related information.

Creates the Weather and Earth System Modeling and Data Assimilation Workforce Innovation Program to support education, recruitment, and workforce development in weather modeling and data assimilation, emphasizing artificial intelligence and machine learning.

Requires the Under Secretary to develop and update a workforce human capital plan and report on the program's effectiveness.

Authorizes appropriations for these initiatives, including $40 million annually from 2025 to 2030 for the workforce program and additional funding for NOAA's weather research activities.

Official name: NO FAKES Act of 2024
Casual name: NO FAKES Act of 2024
Short summary: Establishes rights for individuals over digital replicas of their voice or likeness, requiring consent for creation and use. Imposes liability for unauthorized replication. Provides safe harbors for online services complying with takedown notices. Limits preemption of existing state laws.
Long summary:
Establishes the right for individuals and right holders to authorize the use of their voice or visual likeness in digital replicas, treating it as a property right that is licensable but not assignable during the individual's life.

Allows post-mortem transfer and licensing, enabling rights to be inherited and renewed under specific conditions.

Imposes liability for unauthorized production, distribution, or public display of digital replicas without consent, with certain exclusions for news, historical, and public interest uses.

Provides safe harbors for online services that act promptly to remove unauthorized replicas upon notification.

Preempts conflicting state laws, except for those existing before January 2, 2025, or those addressing sexually explicit or election-related replicas.

Applies only to conduct and contracts occurring after the enactment date, while granting rights retroactively to deceased individuals' estates.

Official name: Secure Artificial Intelligence Act of 2024
Casual name: Secure A.I. Act
Short summary: Establishes a voluntary database to track and process identified AI security and safety incidents and risks, and requires the facilitation of research to develop new and evaluate existing safety guidelines and processes.
Long summary:
Establishes a voluntary database to track AI security and safety incidents, requiring the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to develop mechanisms for sharing incident information while preserving confidentiality.

Mandates NIST to update the National Vulnerability Database to incorporate AI security vulnerabilities and identify characteristics that make the database unsuitable for certain AI vulnerabilities.

Prohibits employer retaliation against employees who report AI incidents, allowing legal recourse for aggrieved parties.

Directs the establishment of best practices for addressing AI supply chain risks, involving multiple stakeholders.

Established the Artificial Intelligence Security Center within the National Security Agency to facilitate AI security research, guidance development, and ensure the availability of test-beds for secure AI research.

Official name: Department of Energy Artificial Intelligence Act of 2024
Casual name: Department of Energy AI Act
Short summary: Establishes a Department of Energy program to advance AI research and development across various sectors, including security, energy, and infrastructure. Supports AI innovation, data management, facility upgrades, risk management, and partnerships. Allocates $300 million annually from 2025-2030.
Long summary:
Establishes a cross-cutting AI research and development program within the Department of Energy to advance AI tools, systems, and capabilities.

Mandates coordination across relevant offices such as the Office of Science and the Office of Energy Efficiency to carry out this program.

Requires financial assistance for research in areas like AI application in simulations, data analysis, and energy-efficient computing.

Supports technology transfer and the use of high-performance computing infrastructure to develop advanced AI systems.

Establishes data center testbeds to improve energy efficiency in AI training and inference.

Encourages partnerships with federal and private entities to enhance AI research and development.

Emphasizes ethical considerations, cybersecurity, and data privacy in AI projects.

Allocates $300 million annually from 2025 to 2030 for these initiatives.

Requires an assessment of energy security related to data centers and computing resources, with recommendations for policy changes.

Official name: Protecting Patients from Deceptive Drug Ads Online Act
Casual name: Protecting Patients from Deceptive Drug Ads Online Act
Short summary: Amends the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to penalize false or misleading drug communications by social media influencers and healthcare providers. Requires guidance and regulations for telehealth providers. Authorizes FDA to conduct AI-enhanced market surveillance of drug promotions.
Long summary:
Amends the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act to impose civil penalties on social media influencers and healthcare providers who make false or misleading communications about prescription drugs.

Mandates the issuance of guidance within 180 days to define false or misleading communications and outline compliance procedures.

Expands the definition of "manufacturer, packer, or distributor" to include telehealth providers involved in drug advertising.

Requires reporting of payments related to drug promotion to the Secretary of Health and Human Services.

Authorizes market surveillance activities using AI to monitor prescription drug promotions on social media and coordinate efforts with the Federal Trade Commission.

Allocates $15 million annually for fiscal years 2025 to 2029 to support these activities.

Establishes a process for notifying drug manufacturers of non-compliant communications by influencers or healthcare providers.

Ensures the severability of the Act's provisions, maintaining validity even if parts are invalidated.

Official name: SPEED through Screening Act
Casual name: SPEED through Screening Act
Short summary: Requires the TSA Administrator to develop strategies to reduce patdowns and item divestiture during airport screenings using advanced technologies, including AI. Authorizes $20 million for research and deployment, mandates data collection, and allows publication of anonymized statistics.
Long summary:
Requires the Administrator to submit strategies within one year to reduce patdowns and the need for item divestiture during passenger screening without compromising security.

Considers technological improvements, discrimination reduction among passenger populations, and next-generation screening technologies in developing these strategies.

Mandates a feasibility study on allowing increased volumes of liquids, aerosols, gels, and powders on aircraft without reducing security.

Permits deployment of detection at range technology for screening, subject to feasibility assessments and prioritizes reducing patdowns and enhancing security for vulnerable populations.

Obligates collection and publication of anonymized screening statistics, considering privacy and civil rights, utilizing AI and noninvasive technologies.

Allocates $20 million for fiscal year 2024 to research and deploy improved screening technologies.

Requires annual briefings to congressional committees and a Comptroller General review within two years on the Act's implementation.

Official name: Improving Seniors’ Timely Access to Care Act of 2024
Casual name: Improving Seniors’ Timely Access to Care Act of 2024
Short summary: Requires Medicare Advantage plans to implement electronic prior authorization programs by 2027, ensuring secure data transmission and compliance with technical standards. Mandates transparency in technology use, including AI, and reporting on decision-making, appeals, and access impacts. Establishes enrollee protection standards.
Long summary:
Mandates Medicare Advantage plans, starting January 1, 2027, to implement an electronic prior authorization program for secure electronic transmission of requests and responses, excluding certain formats like facsimiles.

Requires plans, beginning January 1, 2026, to meet transparency requirements, including submitting annual data on prior authorization requests, appeals, and the use of AI technologies in decision-making.

Obligates plans to provide access to authorization criteria and documentation requirements to providers, suppliers, and enrollees upon request.

Empowers the Secretary of Health and Human Services to establish response timeframes for prior authorization requests, potentially as short as 24 hours.

Tasks the Medicare Payment Advisory Commission with analyzing and reporting on prior authorization use and recommending improvements.

Directs the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services to study and report on real-time decision processes and the impact of AI on patient access, especially for rural and low-income beneficiaries.

Official name: Providing Individuals Various Opportunities for Technical Training to Build a Skills-Based Cyber Workforce Act of 2024
Casual name: Cyber PIVOTT Act
Short summary: Expands CISA education and training programs to community colleges and technical schools, focusing on cyber-relevant fields, including AI. Provides scholarships, internships, and service obligations. Requires participation in skills-based exercises and aims to enroll 10,000 students annually within ten years.
Long summary:
Amends the Homeland Security Act of 2002 to establish the "PIVOTT Program" for expanding cybersecurity education and training.

Requires CISA to partner with community colleges and technical schools to offer programs and internships aiming to build a skills-based cyber workforce.

Provides full scholarships to eligible students, covering all educational and associated costs.

Mandates a two-year service obligation in a cyber role for program participants, with exceptions for military service.

Specifies eligible institutions and program components, including skills-based exercises and internships.

Directs CISA to conduct outreach initiatives and host recruitment fairs.

Establishes program completion benefits, including access to a cyber training database and additional certification opportunities.

Sets terms for scholarship repayment if obligations are not met and allows for waiver of repayments in cases of hardship.

Outlines enrollment targets and requires reporting on program enrollment and CISA's education and training initiatives.

Official name: Technology in the Parks Act of 2024
Casual name: Technology in the Parks Act of 2024
Short summary:
Amends the Communications Act of 1934 to extend E-rate support to local parks. 

Authorizes the Secretary of Labor to establish a grant program for technology training programs directed towards needy families in local parks.
Long summary:
Amends the Communications Act of 1934 to extend E-rate support to local parks, allowing them to access discounted telecommunications services for technology training programs.

Requires the creation of internet safety regulations for parks receiving such services to ensure cybersecurity standards similar to those for schools and libraries.

Mandates the Secretary of Labor to initiate a grant program for technology training directed towards needy families in local parks, focusing on topics like artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, and web development.

Limits grant coverage to 10% of the training programs' operating costs and to a duration of no longer than two years.

Official name: AI Development Practices Act of 2024
Casual name: AI Development Practices Act of 2024
Short summary: Amends the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act to require developing voluntary AI development guidelines. Focuses on risk management, communication practices, and collaboration. Involves public reporting, security measures, and international standards alignment. Mandates committee briefings 18 months post-enactment.
Long summary:
Amends the National Institute of Standards and Technology Act to require the development of voluntary guidelines for AI system development, release, and assessment.

Mandates the Director of NIST to collaborate with public and private organizations to create guidance on AI risk tolerances and communication of system characteristics.

Requires categorization of practices related to security, safety, fairness, privacy, and other attributes of AI systems.

Obligates the Director to update the voluntary risk management framework for trustworthy AI systems.

Prohibits prescribing specific technologies; mandates reliance on international standards and industry best practices.

Requires collaboration with public, industry, and academic entities for guidance development.

Demands a report to Congress within 18 months on new materials or systems produced and policy recommendations for private-public sector coordination.

Defines "artificial intelligence red teaming" and "artificial intelligence system" for clarity.

Official name: Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2025, Title V, Section 504 ("National security procedures to address certain risks and threats relating to artificial intelligence")
Casual name: Intelligence Authorization Act FY 2025, Title V, Section 504 ("National security procedures to address certain risks and threats relating to artificial intelligence")
Short summary: Requires the President to develop procedures for notifying the government of AI-related security risks. Mandates government threat briefings to AI vendors. Obligates a briefing to Congress on agency responsibilities, industry outreach, and public education plans regarding AI risks.
Long summary:
Mandates the President to develop procedures within 180 days to enable vendors, users, and researchers to notify the U.S. Government of AI-related security and biosecurity risks, including malicious software development, system compromise, and foreign malign influence.

Requires federal elements to provide threat briefings to AI vendors about foreign exploitation and threats.

Obligates the President to brief Congress on the procedures, specifying responsible federal agencies and outlining industry outreach and public education plans.

Utilizes existing laws and federal guidelines for research, stakeholder engagement, and risk management frameworks related to AI systems.

Official name: Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2025, Title V, Section 505 ("Establishment of Artificial Intelligence Security Center")
Casual name: Intelligence Authorization Act FY 2025, Title V, Section 505 ("Establishment of Artificial Intelligence Security Center")
Short summary: Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Security Center within the NSA. Provides a research test bed, develops counter-AI guidance, promotes secure AI adoption, and coordinates with NIST. Ensures access to proprietary models for qualified researchers and sets usage terms.
Long summary:
Establishes an Artificial Intelligence Security Center within the National Security Agency's Cybersecurity Collaboration Center.

Requires the provision of a research test bed for AI security research to private sector and academic researchers, including secure access to proprietary third-party models with vendor consent.

Develops guidance to prevent or mitigate counter-artificial intelligence techniques.

Promotes secure AI adoption practices for national security systems and the defense industrial base.

Coordinates with the Artificial Intelligence Safety Institute of the National Institute of Standards and Technology.

Mandates terms of usage for researcher access to the test bed, ensuring limited publication to protect classified or proprietary information.

Ensures test bed availability to federal agencies on a cost-recovery basis.

Leverages existing infrastructure and resources for AI security research.

Establishes mechanisms, including contractual incentives, for providing access to proprietary models for qualified researchers.

Official name: Intelligence Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2025, Title V, Section 510 ("Management of artificial intelligence security risks")
Casual name: Intelligence Authorization Act FY 2025, Title V, Section 510 ("Management of artificial intelligence security risks")
Short summary: Requires the National Institute of Standards and Technology to update vulnerability management processes for AI security risks. Establishes a voluntary database to track AI security and safety incidents. Evaluates standards for AI vulnerability reporting. Encourages best practices for AI supply chain risks.
Long summary:
Mandates the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology to update the National Vulnerability Database to incorporate AI security vulnerabilities within 180 days.

Requires the development of a voluntary database to track AI security and safety incidents, ensuring confidentiality and standardized reporting formats.

Instructs the Director to evaluate AI incidents for potential inclusion in the database, prioritizing critical infrastructure and high-impact cases.

Directs the Director of the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency to update the Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures Program to address AI security vulnerabilities.

Initiates a multi-stakeholder evaluation of consensus standards for AI vulnerability reporting, with a report due to Congress within 180 days.

Requires collaboration to develop best practices for managing AI supply chain risks, focusing on data collection, documentation, and the use of open-source and proprietary datasets.

Official name: Sec. 103, Supporting Affordability and Fairness with Every Bet Act
Casual name: Sec. 103, Supporting Affordability and Fairness with Every Bet Act
Short summary: Prohibits sports wagering operators from using AI to track individual wagers, target promotions, or create gambling products like microbets. Requires operators to follow strict data security and consumer protection standards, including preventing underage gambling and ensuring contest integrity.
Long summary:
Prohibits sports wagering operators from using artificial intelligence to track individual wagers, target specific individuals with offers or promotions, or create gambling products like microbets.

Requires sports wagering operators to ensure data security and to take reasonable steps to prevent unauthorized access or dissemination of wagering and customer data.

Mandates real-time or near-real-time sharing of anonymized sports wagering data with the State regulatory entity.

Imposes strict consumer protections, including restrictions on AI use, limits on deposits, and mandatory affordability checks for high-value wagers.

Establishes comprehensive standards for licensing, recordkeeping, and employee background checks to ensure the integrity and accountability of sports wagering operators.

Requires cooperation with investigations by regulatory authorities and sports organizations, and mandates robust internal controls for operators.

Prohibits advertising targeting problem gamblers or individuals ineligible to wager, and restricts broadcast times and content. 

Enforces restrictions to maintain contest integrity and prevent betting on certain events and by prohibited individuals.

Official name: STAND with Taiwan Act of 2024, Section 11 ("Prohibition on investments by United States financial institutions that benefit the Government of the People's Republic of China or the Chinese Communist Party")
Casual name: STAND with Taiwan Act of 2024, Section 11 ("Prohibition on investments by United States financial institutions that benefit the Government of the People's Republic of China or the Chinese Communist Party")
Short summary: Prohibits U.S. financial institutions from investing in entities tied to China's government or military and sectors like AI, robotics, and quantum computing, as identified in strategic plans like "Made in China 2025" and the "14th Five Year Smart Manufacturing Development Plan."
Long summary:
Prohibits United States financial institutions from making investments benefiting the Government of the People's Republic of China or the Chinese Communist Party.

Mandates the Secretary of the Treasury to enforce this prohibition within three days of a covered determination.

Defines prohibited investments as monetary investments to entities owned or controlled by the Chinese government or military.

Includes investments that benefit priority industrial sectors identified in Chinese strategic plans, such as "Made in China 2025" and the "14th Five Year Smart Manufacturing Development Plan."

Specifies prohibited sectors including artificial intelligence, machine learning, and robotics.

Defines "United States financial institution" to include investment companies, private equity firms, venture capital companies, and hedge funds that qualify as United States persons.

Official name: Modernizing Wildfire Safety and Prevention Act of 2024, Section 405 ("Joint Office of the Fire Environment Center (Report Recommendations 104, 105, 106)")
Casual name: Modernizing Wildfire Safety and Prevention Act of 2024, Section 405 ("Joint Office of the Fire Environment Center (Report Recommendations 104, 105, 106)")
Short summary: Establishes a Joint Office to test AI/ML for fire management, supporting risk modeling and resource allocation. Requires collaboration with local entities and data sharing. Ensures development of decision support tools and air quality monitoring, with a $150 million annual budget.
Long summary:
Establishes the Joint Office of the Fire Environment Center within one year, under the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.

Requires the Data Services branch to test AI and machine learning technologies to aid fire management, suggesting resources based on real-time fire conditions.

Mandates the provision of science-based, data-rich services for fire management, including predictive and decision-support tools.

Develops a technological common operating environment to integrate air quality data and other public health information.

Forms a 12-member board governing the Joint Office, including federal and non-federal wildfire community representatives.

Empowers the Joint Office to contract with government and private entities for necessary supplies and services.

Authorizes $150 million annually from fiscal years 2025 to 2034 for the Joint Office's operations.

Official name: Health Equity and Accountability Act of 2024, Sec 1013 ("Task Force on Preventing Bias in AI and Algorithms")
Casual name: Health Equity and Accountability Act of 2024, Sec 1013 ("Task Force on Preventing Bias in AI and Algorithms")
Short summary: Establishes a Task Force to provide guidance on preventing AI and algorithmic bias in healthcare. Requires examination of AI's impact on civil rights, health disparities, and outcomes. Mandates a report to Congress and public comment period.
Long summary:
Establishes the Task Force on Preventing AI and Algorithmic Bias in Health Care within 30 days to guide the development of AI technologies that do not exacerbate health disparities.

Composes the Task Force of various health and public officials, including experts on health equity and minority health.

Mandates the Task Force to examine AI and algorithms in healthcare, identifying risks related to civil rights, civil liberties, and discriminatory bias.

Requires submission of a report to Congress within one year detailing findings and recommendations for safe AI implementation in healthcare.

Obliges the Task Force to publish the report on the Department of Health and Human Services' website.

Initiates a public comment period of at least 90 days on the Task Force’s duties and activities.

Allows the Chairperson to appoint additional staff without adherence to certain civil service laws, ensuring flexibility in task force operations.

Official name: Fix Our Forests Act, Section 303 ("Public-private wildfire technology deployment and demonstration partnership")
Casual name: Fix Our Forests Act, Section 303 ("Public-private wildfire technology deployment and demonstration partnership")
Short summary: Establishes a pilot program for deploying innovative wildfire technologies. Prioritizes AI, quantum sensing, and other emerging technologies. Requires reporting on technology deployment, costs, and recommendations for scalable adoption. Connects private entities with federal agencies for real-time testing.
Long summary:
Establishes a deployment and demonstration pilot program for innovative wildfire technologies, including AI, within one year.

Incorporates the program into the National Wildfire Coordinating Group and advances key technology areas like remote sensing and interoperable data.

Requires coordination between selected private entities and federal agencies for real-time technology testing during wildfire mitigation.

Prioritizes entities involved in the Fire Weather Testbed or developing emerging technologies like AI and quantum sensing.

Mandates public outreach to invite applications from entities to demonstrate technologies.

Obligates annual reporting to Congress on program participants, technologies demonstrated, costs, outreach, and recommendations for technology adoption.

Specifies the program's authority will terminate seven years after enactment.

Official name: Fix Our Forests Act, Section 102 ("Fireshed Center")
Casual name: Fix Our Forests Act, Section 102 ("Fireshed Center")
Short summary: Establishes a Fireshed Center to assess and predict fire and smoke using AI and data tools. Promotes coordination, data sharing, and streamlined processes among federal agencies and other entities. Disseminates AI-based decision support products for fire risk reduction and recovery.
Long summary:
Establishes a Fireshed Center through collaboration between the Forest Service and the U.S. Geological Survey, involving multiple federal agencies.

Appoints a Director from the U.S. Geological Survey or the Forest Service for a term of up to seven years.

Utilizes artificial intelligence and data tools to assess and predict fire and smoke at the wildland-urban interface for improved management, risk reduction, and recovery.

Promotes data sharing and coordination across federal, state, local, tribal, and private sectors.

Reduces duplication and fragmentation in predictive services and decision support related to wildland fires.

Streamlines procurement and cybersecurity processes.

Develops and disseminates publicly accessible data, models, and technologies for fire risk reduction and recovery planning.

Maintains the Fireshed Registry and supports its operations with personnel and services from the U.S. Geological Survey and the Forest Service.

Official name: American Medical Innovation and Investment Act of 2024 (Sec. 5)
Casual name: American Medical Innovation and Investment Act of 2024 (Sec. 5)
Short summary: Requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to issue guidance by January 1, 2026, on Medicare payment requirements for remote monitoring devices with AI components that transmit data to healthcare providers for patient management and treatment.
Long summary:
Mandates the Secretary of Health and Human Services to issue guidance on Medicare payment requirements for remote monitoring devices with AI components by January 1, 2026. 

Specifies that the devices, including continuous glucose monitors, must use AI components like continuous adjustment. 

Requires that these devices transmit information to healthcare providers for management and treatment purposes.

Official name: Combat Chinese Economic Aggression Act of 2024, Section 4 ("Protection of covered sectors.")
Casual name: Combat Chinese Economic Aggression Act of 2024, Section 4 ("Protection of covered sectors.")
Short summary: Amends the Defense Production Act to restrict U.S. persons from engaging in covered activities involving AI and other sectors with countries of concern. Requires notification of certain activities, mandates regulations, and authorizes penalties for violations.
Long summary:
Amends the Defense Production Act to add Title VIII, focusing on protecting sectors like AI.

Defines "covered activities" involving U.S. persons and foreign entities in countries of concern, including AI-related investments and collaborations.

Mandates the President to publish and annually update a list of technologies posing national security risks.

Empowers the President to prohibit U.S. persons from engaging in certain activities with foreign entities in critical sectors, including AI.

Requires mandatory notifications for covered activities not prohibited, with confidentiality protections for submitted information.

Obligates annual reporting to Congress on notifications, trends, and effectiveness of measures.

Directs the President to issue regulations specifying covered activities and technologies.

Encourages multilateral engagement with U.S. allies to develop comparable mechanisms for managing risks in covered sectors.

Authorizes penalties, including fines and imprisonment, for violations.

Provides for national interest waivers, requiring notification to Congress.

Official name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 402 ("Expanding and improving electronic technologies.")
Casual name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 402 ("Expanding and improving electronic technologies.")
Short summary: Encourages the use of electronic technologies for fisheries data collection, including AI applications. Requires periodic reviews and reports on these technologies. Establishes an innovation prize and advisory panel to advance electronic technologies in fisheries management, focusing on AI and machine learning.
Long summary:
Mandates the use of electronic monitoring and other technologies in fisheries data collection, allowing vessels to avoid carrying observers if conditions are unsafe.

Requires the Secretary to integrate electronic monitoring and reporting technologies into fishery information collection programs and review these systems every five years.

Directs the Comptroller General to assess the National Marine Fisheries Service's electronic monitoring capabilities and recommend improvements.

Establishes an electronic technologies innovation prize to encourage advancements in AI, machine learning, and data management for fisheries.

Creates an advisory panel to guide the development and deployment of electronic technologies in fisheries, including performance standards and data ownership.

Exempts the advisory panel from the Federal Advisory Committee Act.

Allows Councils to independently develop electronic technologies for data collection and management.

Official name: Platform Accountability and Transparency Act, Section 9 ("Rulemaking authority")
Casual name: Platform Accountability and Transparency Act, Section 9 ("Rulemaking authority")
Short summary: Mandates regulations for data provision, public content repositories, and ad disclosure. Compels algorithm reporting and content moderation transparency.
Long summary:
Requires platforms to make available public repositories of highly disseminated content and major public accounts, including content samples and metadata.

Obligates platforms to disclose advertising information, including ad content, targeting parameters, and dissemination metrics, while protecting user privacy.

Requires disclosure of the use of platform algorithms in the recommendation, amplification, or restriction of content and advertising

Compels platforms to report on recommender and ranking algorithms, detailing inputs, objectives, and significant changes, without revealing trade secrets.

Demands transparency in content moderation, with statistics on policy violations, identification methods (including artificial intelligence), and government orders.

Official name: WIOA Performance Accountability Improvement Act, Section 2, Subsection (c) ("Evaluation of State programs.")
Casual name: WIOA Performance Accountability Improvement Act, Section 2, Subsection (c) ("Evaluation of State programs.")
Short summary: Amends the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act to permit states to use machine learning and advanced analytics for improving program operations, outcomes, and evaluation, enhancing data-driven decision-making in state programs.
Long summary:
Amends the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act to require states to use data for analyses and ongoing evaluations of programs.

Allows states to use machine learning or other advanced analytics to enhance program operations, outcomes, and identify areas needing further evaluation.

Official name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 401 ("Data modernization.")
Casual name: Sustaining America’s Fisheries for the Future Act of 2024, Title IV, Section 401 ("Data modernization.")
Short summary: Aligns fisheries data modernization with NOAA's AI Strategy for efficiency.
Long summary:
Aligns the national strategic plan for fisheries data with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's (NOAA) Artificial Intelligence Strategy to enhance efficiency and interoperability.

Requires the Secretary, through the Assistant Administrator for Fisheries, to provide a strategic plan to Congress within 180 days, detailing goals, implementation schedules, budgets, stakeholder engagement, and system development.

Solicits expertise from data management and technology experts both within and outside the government to support the strategic plan.

Mandates the Secretary to report annually to Congress on progress in implementing the fisheries data modernization efforts.

Official name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 203
Casual name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 203
Short summary: Amends the Social Security Act to require behavioral health information disclosure, including provider access, wait times, payment statistics, and denial data. Specifies AI and technology use in denial processes. Aims to enhance transparency and informed choice in behavioral health coverage.
Long summary:
Amends Section 1851(d)(4) of the Social Security Act to include provisions for behavioral health information starting in 2025.

Requires the provision of information on access to in-network behavioral health providers, including average wait times for new patient appointments and data on provider participation agreements.

Mandates disclosure of payment request statistics for mental health or substance use disorder services, specifying the percentage handled by in-network versus out-of-network providers.

Requires reporting on the number and percentage of denials for prior authorization or payment for mental health services compared to non-mental health services, categorized by denial type and service type.

Specifies the use of decision support, AI, machine learning, clinical decision-making, or other technologies in denial processes, with comparisons to similar physical health conditions.

Aims to promote informed choice by enhancing transparency in behavioral health service coverage.

Official name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 201
Casual name: Better Mental Health Care for Americans Act, Sec. 201
Short summary: Amends Medicare Advantage plans to ensure mental health and substance use benefits parity. Mandates standards-based necessity determination and NQTL analyses. Imposes penalties for non-compliance. Extends parity to drug plans. Directs regulation issuance within 18 months. Allocates $10 million for implementation.
Long summary:
Amends Medicare Advantage plans to require parity in mental health and substance use disorder benefits, ensuring financial and treatment limitations are no more restrictive than those for medical benefits.

Mandates MA organizations to determine medical necessity for mental health and substance use benefits based on generally accepted standards.

Requires MA organizations to conduct and submit comparative analyses of nonquantitative treatment limitations (NQTLs) for mental health and substance use disorder benefits.

Imposes penalties for failing to comply with the mental health parity requirements.

Extends parity provisions to prescription drug plans, ensuring consistent application across Medicare Advantage and prescription drug plans.

Directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services to issue implementing regulations within 18 months.

Sets the effective date for amendments to plan years beginning two years post-enactment, with $10 million allocated for implementation funding through the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services.

Official name: Supercomputing for Safer Chemicals Act
Casual name: SUPERSAFE Act
Short summary: Directs the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency to establish a consortium that identifies toxic substances and develops safer alternatives through computational toxicology methods based on AI and machine learning.
Long summary:
Directs the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to establish a consortium representing federal, state, and academic stakeholders. The consortium will use supercomputing and machine learning to develop models that predict adverse health effects from toxic substances, establishing rapid approaches for large-scale identification of toxic substances and the development of safer alternatives.

Requires the Consortium to recommend how the information produced can be used in risk assessments by the EPA, other federal agencies, and industry.

Appropriates funding to the EPA Administrator to carry out the Consortium and requires the Consortium to make its model predictions public and accessible.

Official name: Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act of 2023
Casual name: Block Nuclear Launch by Autonomous Artificial Intelligence Act
Short summary: Bans the use of federal funds to operate autonomous weapons systems which launch nuclear weapons or select targets for nuclear weapons without meaningful human control.
Long summary:
Prohibits the use of federal funds to operate autonomous weapons systems which launch nuclear weapons, or select or engage targets for nuclear weapons, without meaningful human control.

Defines 'meaningful human control' as the ability for humans to control the selection and engagement of targets, as well as the time, location and manner of the use of nuclear weapons.

Official name: Creating Resources for Every American To Experiment with Artificial Intelligence Act of 2023
Casual name: CREATE AI Act of 2023
Short summary: Establishes the National AI Research Resource to provide computational resources, data, educational tools, and testbeds for AI researchers.
Long summary:
Establishes a steering committee to oversee the NAIRR.

Authorizes the National Science Foundation, in coordination with the NAIRR steering committee, to establish the National AI Research Resource.

Creates a NAIRR Program Management Office to manage the NAIRR's day-to-day operations.

Directs the Program Management Office to solicit bids for an Operating Entity for the NAIRR and to oversee that Entity.

Provides for the NAIRR to be resourced with computational resources, data, educational tools and services, and AI testbeds.

Specifies eligibility for access to resources in the NAIRR, including academic, nonprofit, government, and small businesses, among others.

Provides for procedures to establish privacy, ethical, safety and scientific integrity requirements for users of the NAIRR.

Authorizes the NAIRR to charge fees for users at various tiers, including free and subsidized tiers.

Provides for security requirements to prevent unauthorized access and protect research security.

Official name: William M. (Mac) Thornberry National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021, Division A, Title XCIV, Section 9412 ("Industries of the future")
Casual name: Industries of the Future Act of 2020
Short summary: Focuses on investing and developing new industries of the future which are regarded as Artificial Intelligence and quantum information science.  Creates a new council to enforce these new laws. Assigns responsibilities to the President and terminates the act 6 years after implementation.
Long summary:
Provides a multi-step plan in order to ensure that the United States will "continue to lead the world in developing emerging technologies".
Furthers the national interests and security goals of the nation.
Promotes collaboration between existing and new depraments.
Requires the Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy to submit a detailed report regarding current investments, research and development projects, and more regarding the US mission to be involved in the development of creating the industries of the future. 
Defines the industries specifically
Outlines a plan to increase funding and investments to developments in Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Information Science to 10 billion dollars a year by the year 2025.
Instructs the President to create a council called the Industries of the Future Coordination Council to support and guide the Director.
Outlines the membership guidelines and duties of the councils to uphold the objective that the US will lead the world in technology.
Specifies the termination date of the policy to be 6 years from enactment, on Jan 1, 2027.
